title: Amazing Grace
origin: John Newton, 1779 (Olney Hymns) — final verse added anonymously before 1829
note: Newton's spiritual autobiography, written after he left the slave trade. The tune 'New Britain' wasn't paired with it until 1835.
verses:
Amazing grace! How sweet the sound
That saved a wretch like me!
I once was lost, but now am found,
Was blind, but now I see.

'Twas grace that taught my heart to fear,
And grace my fears relieved;
How precious did that grace appear
The hour I first believed!

Through many dangers, toils and snares,
I have already come;
'Tis grace hath brought me safe thus far,
And grace will lead me home.

The Lord has promised good to me,
His word my hope secures;
He will my shield and portion be,
As long as life endures.

Yes, when this flesh and heart shall fail,
And mortal life shall cease;
I shall possess, within the veil,
A life of joy and peace.

The earth shall soon dissolve like snow,
The sun forbear to shine;
But God, who called me here below,
Will be forever mine.

When we've been there ten thousand years,
Bright shining as the sun,
We've no less days to sing God's praise
Than when we first begun.

title: Swing Low, Sweet Chariot
origin: Traditional — composed by Wallace Willis, a freedman in Choctaw Territory, mid-19th century
note: First made famous nationally by the Fisk Jubilee Singers, 1909. The Jordan/chariot imagery is widely read as coded Underground Railroad language.
verses:
Swing low, sweet chariot,
Coming for to carry me home.
Swing low, sweet chariot,
Coming for to carry me home.

I looked over Jordan, and what did I see,
Coming for to carry me home,
A band of angels coming after me,
Coming for to carry me home.

If you get there before I do,
Coming for to carry me home,
Tell all my friends I'm coming too,
Coming for to carry me home.

The brightest day that ever I saw,
Coming for to carry me home,
When Jesus washed my sins away,
Coming for to carry me home.

I'm sometimes up and sometimes down,
Coming for to carry me home,
But still my soul feels heavenly bound,
Coming for to carry me home.

title: Nobody Knows the Trouble I've Seen
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, published 1867 in Slave Songs of the United States
note: Originated during slavery; not published until after the Civil War. Recorded by Mahalia Jackson, Louis Armstrong, Marian Anderson, and many others.
verses:
Nobody knows the trouble I've seen,
Nobody knows my sorrow;
Nobody knows the trouble I've seen,
Glory hallelujah!

Sometimes I'm up, sometimes I'm down,
Oh, yes, Lord!
Sometimes I'm almost to the ground,
Oh, yes, Lord!

Although you see me going 'long so,
Oh, yes, Lord!
I have my trials here below,
Oh, yes, Lord!

If you get there before I do,
Oh, yes, Lord!
Tell all my friends I'm coming too,
Oh, yes, Lord!

title: When the Saints Go Marching In
origin: Traditional gospel song, earliest known recording 1923 (Paramount Jubilee Singers)
note: Became a jazz standard after Louis Armstrong's 1938 recording; later adopted as the New Orleans Saints' anthem.
verses:
Oh, when the saints go marching in,
Oh, when the saints go marching in,
Lord, I want to be in that number
When the saints go marching in.

Oh, when the sun refused to shine,
Oh, when the sun refused to shine,
Lord, I want to be in that number
When the sun refused to shine.

Oh, when they gather round the throne,
Oh, when they gather round the throne,
Lord, I want to be in that number
When they gather round the throne.

Oh, when they crown Him King of kings,
Oh, when they crown Him King of kings,
Lord, I want to be in that number
When they crown Him King of kings.

And on that hallelujah day,
And on that hallelujah day,
Lord, I want to be in that number
On that hallelujah day.

title: Oh! Susanna
origin: Stephen Foster, 1848
note: Foster's first big hit; became an anthem of the 1849 California Gold Rush. Presented here with the verses in standard modern circulation (the original 1848 sheet music included a second verse with racist minstrel-era language that is omitted here).
verses:
I came from Alabama, with my banjo on my knee,
I'm going to Louisiana, my true love for to see.
It rained all night the day I left, the weather it was dry,
The sun so hot I froze to death; Susanna, don't you cry.

Oh, Susanna, oh don't you cry for me,
For I come from Alabama, with my banjo on my knee.

I had a dream the other night, when everything was still;
I thought I saw Susanna, a-coming down the hill.
The buckwheat cake was in her mouth, the tear was in her eye,
Says I, I'm coming from the south, Susanna, don't you cry.

I soon will be in New Orleans, and then I'll look all round,
And when I find Susanna, I will fall upon the ground.
But if I do not find her, this man will surely die,
And when I'm dead and buried, Susanna, don't you cry.

title: John Henry
origin: Traditional American ballad, oral tradition from the 1870s; earliest known printed text c. 1900
note: Ballad of a steel-driving railroad worker who raced a steam drill. One of the most recorded folk songs in American history.
verses:
When John Henry was a little baby,
Sitting on his papa's knee,
He picked up a hammer and a little piece of steel,
Said, "This hammer'll be the death of me, Lord, Lord,
This hammer'll be the death of me."

Now the captain said to John Henry,
"I'm gonna bring that steam drill around,
I'm gonna take that steam drill out on the job,
I'm gonna whop that steel on down, Lord, Lord,
Gonna whop that steel on down."

John Henry told his captain,
"A man ain't nothing but a man,
But before I let that steam drill beat me down,
I'll die with my hammer in my hand, Lord, Lord,
I'll die with my hammer in my hand."

Now the man that invented the steam drill,
Thought he was mighty fine,
But John Henry drove his steel fifteen feet,
And the steam drill only made nine, Lord, Lord,
The steam drill only made nine.

John Henry hammered in the mountains,
His hammer was striking fire,
But he worked so hard, he broke his poor heart,
He laid down his hammer and he died, Lord, Lord,
He laid down his hammer and he died.

They took John Henry to the graveyard,
And they buried him in the sand,
And every locomotive comes a-roaring by,
Says, "There lies a steel-driving man, Lord, Lord,
There lies a steel-driving man."

title: House of the Rising Sun
origin: Traditional; oldest documented lyrics published 1925 in Adventure magazine (collected by Robert Winslow Gordon)
note: Public domain traditional song — confirmed in the 1964 Animals royalty dispute, where songwriter Dave Van Ronk found the underlying song couldn't be copyrighted, only a specific arrangement of it.
verses:
There is a house in New Orleans
They call the Rising Sun,
And it's been the ruin of many a poor girl,
And me, oh God, for one.

If I had listened to what my mama said,
I'd be at home today,
But I was young and foolish, poor girl,
Let a gambler lead me astray.

My mother, she's a tailor,
She sews those old blue jeans,
My father, he's a gambling man,
Drinks down in New Orleans.

Go tell my baby sister,
Not to do as I have done,
To shun that house in New Orleans,
They call the Rising Sun.

Well, I'm going back to New Orleans,
My race is almost run,
Going to spend the rest of my days
Beneath that Rising Sun.

title: Frankie and Johnny
origin: Traditional American murder ballad, based on an 1899 St. Louis shooting; familiar lyric form published 1912
note: Inspired by a real case: Frankie Baker shot her lover in a St. Louis rooming house in 1899. Over 250 documented lyric variants exist; the version below follows the widely circulated folk text.
verses:
Frankie and Johnny were lovers,
O Lordy, how they could love,
They swore to be true to each other,
Just as true as the stars above,
He was her man, but he done her wrong.

Frankie and Johnny went walking,
Johnny had a brand new suit,
Frankie paid a hundred dollars
Just to make her man look cute,
He was her man, but he done her wrong.

Johnny said, "I've got to leave you,
But I won't be very long,
Don't you wait up for me, honey,
Nor worry while I'm gone,"
He was her man, but he done her wrong.

Frankie went down to the corner,
Stopped in to buy her some beer,
Says to the fat bartender,
"Has my Johnny man been here?"
He was her man, but he done her wrong.

"Well, I ain't going to tell you no story,
Ain't going to tell you no lie,
Johnny went by about an hour ago
With a girl named Nellie Bly,
He is your man, but he's doing you wrong."

Frankie went home in a hurry,
She didn't go there for fun,
She hurried home to get ahold
Of Johnny's shooting gun,
He was her man, but he done her wrong.

title: Careless Love
origin: Traditional; earliest documented use turn of the 20th century, popularized by W.C. Handy in the early 1920s
note: A staple of Buddy Bolden's New Orleans band and one of the most-covered pieces in blues and jazz — recorded by Bessie Smith, Fats Domino, and countless others.
verses:
Love, oh love, oh careless love,
Love, oh love, oh careless love,
Oh, it's love, oh love, oh careless love,
Just see what love has done to me.

Sorrow, sorrow to my heart,
Sorrow, sorrow to my heart,
Oh, it's sorrow, sorrow to my heart,
When me and my true love must part.

It's a pity that we ever met,
It's a pity that we ever met,
It's a pity that we ever met,
For those good times we'll never forget.

I cried last night and the night before,
I cried last night and the night before,
I cried last night and the night before,
Going to cry tonight and I'll cry no more.

title: Rock Island Line
origin: Traditional railroad work song, documented as early as 1929; first recorded 1934 by Lomax at an Arkansas prison farm
note: Made famous by Lead Belly, whose version launched Lonnie Donegan's 1956 skiffle hit — one of the direct sparks for the British skiffle scene that fed into early British rock and roll.
verses:
Oh, the Rock Island Line is a mighty good road,
Oh, the Rock Island Line is the road to ride,
Oh, the Rock Island Line is a mighty good road,
If you want to ride it, got to ride it like you find it,
Get your ticket at the station on the Rock Island Line.

Jesus died to save our sins,
Glory to God, we're gonna meet Him again.
I may be right and I may be wrong,
I know you're gonna miss me when I'm gone.

title: Red River Valley
origin: Traditional North American folk song, documented in Canadian provinces before 1896; earliest print appearance 1879 (as "Billy's Parting")
note: One of the Western Writers of America's Top 100 Western songs of all time. Likely originated as a farewell song tied to the 1870 Red River Rebellion in Manitoba before migrating south and becoming known as a Texas cowboy song.
verses:
From this valley they say you are going,
We will miss your bright eyes and sweet smile,
For they say you are taking the sunshine
That has brightened our pathway a while.

Come and sit by my side if you love me,
Do not hasten to bid me adieu,
Just remember the Red River Valley
And the cowboy that has loved you so true.

I've been thinking a long time, my darling,
Of the sweet words you never would say,
Now, alas, must my fond hopes all vanish,
For they say you are going away.

Won't you think of the valley you're leaving,
Oh, how lonely and sad it will be,
Oh, think of the fond heart you're breaking,
And the grief you are causing to me.

title: Stagolee
origin: Traditional murder ballad based on an 1895 St. Louis shooting; earliest published text 1911 in the Journal of American Folk-Lore
note: The ballad behind "Stagger Lee" — one of the most recorded songs in American music, covered by Lloyd Price, the Grateful Dead, Bob Dylan, Nick Cave, and Wilson Pickett, among 200+ versions. Text below is the earliest documented printing.
verses:
Stagolee shot Bully; Bully fell down on de flo',
Bully cry out: "Dat fohty-fo' hurts me so."
Stagolee done killed dat Bully now.

Sent for de wagon, wagon didn't come,
Loaded down wid pistols an' all dat gatlin' gun,
Stagolee done kill dat Bully now.

Some giv' a nickel, some giv' a dime,
I didn't give a red copper cent, 'cause he's no friend o' mine,
Stagolee done kill dat Bully now.

Carried po' Bully to cemetary, people standin' 'round,
When preacher say Amen, lay po' body down,
Stagolee done kill dat Bully now.

Fohty dollah coffin, eighty dollah hack,
Carried po' man to cemetary but failed to bring him back,
Ev'y body been dodgin' Stagolee.

title: The Maid Freed from the Gallows
origin: Traditional English/Scottish/American ballad, Child Ballad No. 95, compiled by Francis James Child in English and Scottish Popular Ballads (1882–1898)
note: One of the oldest ballads in the English-speaking folk tradition, with hundreds of variants worldwide. Led Zeppelin adapted it (via Lead Belly's "Gallis Pole" and Fred Gerlach's version) into "Gallows Pole" on Led Zeppelin III.
verses:
O good Lord Judge, and sweet Lord Judge,
Peace for a little while!
Methinks I see my own father,
Come riding by the stile.

"Oh father, oh father, a little of your gold,
And likewise of your fee!
To keep my body from yonder grave,
And my neck from the gallows-tree."

"None of my gold now you shall have,
Nor likewise of my fee;
For I am come to see you hanged,
And hanged you shall be."

O good Lord Judge, and sweet Lord Judge,
Peace for a little while!
Methinks I see my own mother,
Come riding by the stile.
(The mother, brother, and sister each answer the same as the father.)

O good Lord Judge, and sweet Lord Judge,
Peace for a little while!
Methinks I see my own true-love,
Come riding by the stile.

"Oh true-love, oh true-love, a little of your gold,
And likewise of your fee,
To save my body from yonder grave,
And my neck from the gallows-tree."

"Some of my gold now you shall have,
And likewise of my fee,
For I am come to see you saved,
And saved you shall be."

title: In the Pines
origin: Traditional Appalachian ballad dating to at least the 1870s; first documented by folk song collector Cecil Sharp in Kentucky, 1917
note: Also known as "Black Girl" — the traditional song behind Lead Belly's 1940s recordings and, decades later, Nirvana's "Where Did You Sleep Last Night" on MTV Unplugged. This is the older, widely-circulated composite text rather than any single artist's specific arrangement.
verses:
Black girl, black girl, don't lie to me,
Tell me where did you sleep last night.
In the pines, in the pines, where the sun never shines,
I would shiver the whole night through.

Black girl, black girl, where will you go?
I'm going where the cold wind blows.
In the pines, in the pines, where the sun never shines,
I would shiver the whole night through.

My husband was a railroad man,
Killed a mile and a half from here,
His head was found in a driver's wheel,
And his body hasn't never been found.

Black girl, black girl, where will you go?
I'm going where the cold wind blows.
You caused me to weep and you caused me to mourn,
You caused me to leave my home.

title: Midnight Special
origin: Traditional prison work song from the American South; verses first documented in print by Howard Odum, 1905/1911, with the train/light theme appearing by 1923
note: A composite folk song assembled from many floating prison-song verses. Later popularized by Lead Belly and covered by CCR, the Beatles, Van Morrison, Paul McCartney, and dozens more — CCR's 1969 hit version is the one most rock fans know.
verses:
Well, you wake up in the mornin', hear the ding-dong ring,
You go a-marchin' to the table, see the same old thing;
Knife and a fork and a pan,
And if you say anything about it, you're in trouble with the man.

Let the Midnight Special shine her light on me,
Let the Midnight Special shine her ever-lovin' light on me.

Yonder come little Rosie, how in the world do you know?
I can tell her by her apron, and the dress she wore.
Umbrella on her shoulder, piece of paper in her hand,
She goes a-marchin' to the captain, says, "I want my man."

You better walk right, you better not stagger,
You better not fight;
The sheriff will arrest you,
He'll carry you down,
And if the jury finds you guilty,
Penitentiary bound.

title: John the Revelator
origin: Traditional gospel blues call-and-response song; first recorded 1930 by Blind Willie Johnson, credited "Public Domain" on official reissues
note: One of the most influential pre-war gospel blues songs. Covered by Son House, The White Stripes, Depeche Mode, Nick Cave, Beck, R.E.M., and The Blues Brothers. The verses below follow Son House's widely circulated 1965 text.
verses:
Well, who's that writin'?
John the Revelator.
Who's that writin'?
John the Revelator.
Who's that writin'?
John the Revelator.
Wrote the book of the seven seals.

You know God walked down in the cool of the day,
Called Adam by his name,
And he refused to answer,
Because he's naked and ashamed.

You know Christ had twelve apostles,
And three he led away,
He said, "Watch with me one hour,
'till I go yonder and pray."

Christ came on Easter mornin',
Mary and Martha went down to see,
He said, "Go tell my disciples,
To meet me in Galilee."

title: St. James Infirmary Blues
origin: Traditional American blues/jazz standard rooted in the 18th-century English ballad "The Unfortunate Rake"; earliest US sheet music 1925 ("Gambler's Blues"), popularized by Louis Armstrong's 1928 recording
note: Covered by The Animals, Bob Dylan, The White Stripes, The Doors, and Joe Cocker. A 1929 copyright claim by publisher Irving Mills (as "Joe Primrose") was successfully challenged in court, and the song is now widely recognized as traditional and public domain.
verses:
I went down to St. James Infirmary,
Saw my baby there,
Stretched out on a long white table,
So cold, so sweet, so fair.

Let her go, let her go, God bless her,
Wherever she may be,
She can look this wide world over,
But she'll never find a sweet man like me.

Oh, when I die please dress me in straight-lace shoes,
Box-back coat and a Stetson hat,
Put a twenty-dollar gold piece on my watch chain,
So the boys'll know I died standin' pat.

I want six crapshooters for pallbearers,
And a chorus girl to sing me a song,
With a jazz band on my hearse,
To get hot as we march along.

title: See See Rider Blues
origin: Traditional 12-bar blues, first recorded and credited (in part) to Gertrude "Ma" Rainey and Lena Arant, October 1924
note: Also known as "C.C. Rider." Chuck Willis's 1957 rock and roll version is often cited among the records that helped define the emerging genre. Also covered by Elvis Presley, The Animals, The Who, Bruce Springsteen, and Janis Joplin.
verses:
See, see rider, see what you have done,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
Made me love you, now your gal has come.
You made me love you, now your gal has come.

I'm goin' away, baby, won't be back till fall,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
Goin' away, baby, won't be back till fall.
If I find me a good man, I won't be back at all.

I'm gonna buy me a pistol, just as long as I am tall,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
Shoot my man, and catch a cannonball.
If he won't have me, he won't have no gal at all.

title: Trouble in Mind
origin: Blues standard written by jazz pianist Richard M. Jones, first recorded 1924 (Thelma La Vizzo), popularized by Bertha "Chippie" Hill's 1926 recording with Louis Armstrong on cornet
note: A blues-rock and British blues-revival staple, covered by Nina Simone, Big Bill Broonzy, Aretha Franklin, and Fats Domino — a direct thread into the electric blues that shaped 1960s rock.
verses:
Trouble in mind, I'm blue,
But I won't be blue always,
'Cause I know the sun's gonna shine
In my back door someday.

I'm all alone at midnight,
And my lamp is burnin' low,
Never had such trouble in my life before.

I'm goin' down to the river,
I'm gonna take along my rockin' chair,
And if these blues don't leave me,
I'll rock on away from here.

title: Cotton-Eyed Joe
origin: Traditional American folk and fiddle song predating the Civil War; earliest printed version 1882 (Harper and Brothers), collected again by Dorothy Scarborough in 1925
note: One of the oldest documented American folk songs, likely originating among enslaved communities in the antebellum South. A square-dance and rockabilly-adjacent standard that has crossed into country, bluegrass, and novelty pop (Rednex, 1994) — though only the traditional song itself, not any modern arrangement, is public domain.
verses:
Cotton-eyed Joe, Cotton-eyed Joe,
What did make you serve me so,
For to take my gal away from me,
And carry her down to Tennessee?

His eyes were crossed, and his nose was flat,
And his teeth were out, but what of that?
For he was tall, and he was slim,
And so my gal she followed him.
If it hadn't been for Cotton-eyed Joe,
I'd have been married long ago.

No gal so handsome could be found,
Not in all this country round,
With her lips so red and her teeth so white.
If it hadn't been for Cotton-eyed Joe,
I'd have been married long ago.

Don't you remember, don't you know,
Don't you remember Cotton-eyed Joe?
I'd have been married forty years ago,
If it hadn't been for Cotton-eyed Joe.

title: Nobody Knows You When You're Down and Out
origin: Blues standard written and copyrighted by vaudevillian Jimmie Cox in 1923; popularized by Bessie Smith's 1929 recording
note: Famously covered by Derek and the Dominos (with Duane Allman) in 1970 and by Eric Clapton solo on his 1992 MTV Unplugged album, alongside versions by Lead Belly, Janis Joplin, the Allman Brothers, and B.B. King.
verses:
Once I lived the life of a millionaire,
Spendin' my money, I didn't have a care,
I carried my friends out for a good time,
Buying bootleg liquor, champagne and wine.

When I began to fall so low,
I didn't have a friend and no place to go,
So if I ever get my hand on a dollar again,
I'm gonna hold on to it 'til them eagles grin.

Nobody knows you, when you're down and out,
In my pocket not one penny,
And my friends I haven't any.

But if I ever get on my feet again,
Then everybody wants to be my long-lost friend,
It's mighty strange, without a doubt,
Nobody knows you when you're down and out.

title: Drunken Sailor
origin: Traditional sea shanty; melody printed 1824 (Cole's Selection of Favourite Cotillions, Baltimore), shanty documented aboard ships by an 1839 New London whaling-voyage account
note: A 'stamp-and-go' shanty sung for hauling in unison, possibly dating to the 1820s or earlier. One of the few shanties permitted in the Royal Navy. No individual author was ever identified; the song predates recorded music entirely.
verses:
What shall we do with a drunken sailor?
What shall we do with a drunken sailor?
What shall we do with a drunken sailor,
Early in the morning?

Way hay and up she rises,
Way hay and up she rises,
Way hay and up she rises,
Early in the morning.

Put him in the long boat till he's sober,
Put him in the long boat till he's sober,
Put him in the long boat till he's sober,
Early in the morning.

Shave his belly with a rusty razor,
Shave his belly with a rusty razor,
Shave his belly with a rusty razor,
Early in the morning.

Put him in the scuppers with a hosepipe on him,
Put him in the scuppers with a hosepipe on him,
Put him in the scuppers with a hosepipe on him,
Early in the morning.

That's what we do with a drunken sailor,
That's what we do with a drunken sailor,
That's what we do with a drunken sailor,
Early in the morning.

title: Shenandoah
origin: Traditional; originated with Missouri River voyageurs and fur traders early 19th century, adopted as a capstan shanty by deep-water sailors by the mid-1800s; first printed as "Shenadore" in The New Dominion Monthly, 1876
note: Captain Robert Chamblet Adams reported first hearing it around 1850. Began as a river/land song about the Missouri before going to sea; now among the most beloved American melodies, no author ever identified.
verses:
Oh Shenandoah, I long to hear you,
Away, you rolling river,
Oh Shenandoah, I long to hear you,
Away, I'm bound away,
'Cross the wide Missouri.

Oh Shenandoah, I love your daughter,
Away, you rolling river,
For her I'd cross your roaming water,
Away, I'm bound away,
'Cross the wide Missouri.

'Tis seven years since last I've seen you,
Away, you rolling river,
'Tis seven years since last I've seen you,
Away, I'm bound away,
'Cross the wide Missouri.

Oh Shenandoah, I'm bound to leave you,
Away, you rolling river,
Oh Shenandoah, I'll not deceive you,
Away, I'm bound away,
'Cross the wide Missouri.

title: Barbara Allen
origin: Traditional Scottish/English ballad, Child Ballad No. 84; earliest reference in Samuel Pepys's diary, 2 January 1666; first printed by Allan Ramsay in Tea-Table Miscellany, 1740
note: Possibly the most widely collected folk song in the English language, with hundreds of variants across Britain, Ireland, and North America. The text below follows the widely circulated composite American version.
verses:
In Scarlet Town, where I was born,
There was a fair maid dwellin',
Made every youth cry well-a-day,
And her name was Barbara Allen.

All in the merry month of May,
When green buds they were swellin',
Young Jemmy Grove on his deathbed lay,
For love of Barbara Allen.

He sent his servant to her door,
To the town where she was dwellin',
"O haste and come to my master's call,
If your name be Barbara Allen."

So slowly, slowly she got up,
And slowly she drew nigh him,
And all she said when there she came:
"Young man, I think you're dying."

"O mother, mother, make my bed,
O make it soft and narrow;
Sweet William died for me today,
I'll die for him tomorrow."

They buried her in the old churchyard,
And William's grave was nigh her,
And from his heart grew a red, red rose,
And from her heart a briar.

They grew and grew to the steeple top,
Till they could grow no higher,
And there they twined in a true love's knot,
The red rose round the briar.

title: Streets of Laredo
origin: Traditional cowboy ballad descended from the late-18th-century Irish song "The Unfortunate Rake"; first published 1910 in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads
note: Also known as "The Cowboy's Lament." Same ballad family as "St. James Infirmary Blues" — both descend from "The Unfortunate Rake." Frank H. Maynard, a Kansas cowboy, claimed to have written this version in 1876.
verses:
As I walked out in the streets of Laredo,
As I walked out in Laredo one day,
I spied a young cowboy wrapped up in white linen,
Wrapped up in white linen as cold as the clay.

"I see by your outfit that you are a cowboy,"
These words he did say as I boldly stepped by,
"Come sit down beside me and hear my sad story,
I was shot in the breast and I know I must die."

"It was once in the saddle I used to go dashing,
It was once in the saddle I used to go gay,
First down to the dram-house and then to the card-house,
Got shot in the breast and I'm dying today."

"Get six jolly cowboys to carry my coffin,
Get six pretty maidens to bear up my pall,
Put bunches of roses all over my coffin,
Put roses to deaden the clods as they fall."

"Oh, beat the drum slowly and play the fife lowly,
Play the dead march as you carry me along,
Take me to the green valley and lay the sod o'er me,
For I'm a young cowboy and I know I've done wrong."

We beat the drum slowly and played the fife lowly,
And bitterly wept as we bore him along,
For we all loved our comrade, so brave, young and handsome,
We all loved our comrade although he'd done wrong.

title: Man of Constant Sorrow
origin: First published 1913 as "Farewell Song" in a songbook by Dick Burnett, a partially blind fiddler of Monticello, Kentucky; retitled by Emry Arthur's 1928 recording
note: Burnett himself wasn't sure he wrote it — "I think I got the ballad from somebody — I dunno." Carried into the folk revival by the Stanley Brothers and Bob Dylan (1962 debut album), and to a new century by O Brother, Where Art Thou? (2000).
verses:
I am a man of constant sorrow,
I've seen trouble all my days;
I'll bid farewell to old Kentucky,
The place where I was born and raised.

For six long years I've been in trouble,
No pleasure here on earth I've found;
For in this world I'm bound to ramble,
I have no friends to help me now.

It's fare thee well, my own true lover,
I never expect to see you again;
For I'm bound to ride that northern railroad,
Perhaps I'll die upon this train.

You may bury me in some deep valley,
For many years where I may lay;
Then you may learn to love another,
While I am sleeping in my grave.

Maybe your friends think I'm just a stranger,
My face you never will see no more;
But there is one promise that is given,
I'll meet you on God's golden shore.

title: Danny Boy
origin: Lyrics by English lawyer Frederic Weatherly, written 1910 and set to the traditional Irish melody "Londonderry Air" in 1913
note: Weatherly's 1913 publication places both words and melody safely in the public domain. The tune, "Londonderry Air," was collected in County Londonderry and first published in 1855 — itself traditional and authorless.
verses:
Oh, Danny boy, the pipes, the pipes are calling
From glen to glen, and down the mountain side.
The summer's gone, and all the roses falling,
It's you, it's you must go and I must bide.

But come ye back when summer's in the meadow,
Or when the valley's hushed and white with snow,
It's I'll be here in sunshine or in shadow,
Oh, Danny boy, oh Danny boy, I love you so!

But when ye come, and all the flowers are dying,
If I am dead, as dead I well may be,
Ye'll come and find the place where I am lying,
And kneel and say an Ave there for me.

And I shall hear, though soft you tread above me,
And all my grave will warmer, sweeter be,
For you will bend and tell me that you love me,
And I shall sleep in peace until you come to me!

title: Beautiful Dreamer
origin: Stephen Foster; published posthumously March 1864 by Wm. A. Pond & Co., New York
note: Among the last songs Foster wrote before his death in January 1864, and one of his most enduring — a parlor serenade recorded by Bing Crosby, Roy Orbison, and the Beatles (BBC sessions), among many others.
verses:
Beautiful dreamer, wake unto me,
Starlight and dewdrops are waiting for thee;
Sounds of the rude world, heard in the day,
Lulled by the moonlight have all passed away!

Beautiful dreamer, queen of my song,
List while I woo thee with soft melody;
Gone are the cares of life's busy throng,
Beautiful dreamer, awake unto me!
Beautiful dreamer, awake unto me!

Beautiful dreamer, out on the sea,
Mermaids are chanting the wild lorelei;
Over the streamlet vapors are borne,
Waiting to fade at the bright coming morn.

Beautiful dreamer, beam on my heart,
E'en as the morn on the streamlet and sea;
Then will all clouds of sorrow depart,
Beautiful dreamer, awake unto me!
Beautiful dreamer, awake unto me!

title: Hard Times Come Again No More
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1854 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York (Foster's Melodies No. 28)
note: Foster's plea for empathy with the poor, and one of his most-revived songs — recorded by Bob Dylan, Bruce Springsteen, Johnny Cash, Emmylou Harris, and Mavis Staples. Confirmed public domain by the Library of Congress collections.
verses:
'Tis the song, the sigh of the weary,
Hard times, hard times, come again no more.
Many days you have lingered around my cabin door;
Oh! Hard times come again no more.

While we seek mirth and beauty and music light and gay,
There are frail forms fainting at the door;
Though their voices are silent, their pleading looks will say
Oh! Hard times come again no more.

There's a pale drooping maiden who toils her life away,
With a worn heart whose better days are o'er:
Though her voice would be merry, 'tis sighing all the day,
Oh! Hard times come again no more.

'Tis a sigh that is wafted across the troubled wave,
'Tis a wail that is heard upon the shore,
'Tis a dirge that is murmured around the lowly grave,
Oh! Hard times come again no more.

title: Wayfaring Stranger
origin: Traditional American folk hymn of unknown authorship, early 19th century; earliest known publication 1858 in Joseph Bever's The Christian Songster (Dayton, Ohio)
note: Also known as "Poor Wayfaring Stranger." May trace to an older German hymn tradition. Carried through the 20th century by Burl Ives, Emmylou Harris, Johnny Cash, and Ed Sheeran-era revivals — the underlying song remains authorless and public domain.
verses:
I'm just a poor wayfaring stranger,
Traveling through this world of woe,
Yet there's no sickness, toil, nor danger
In that bright land to which I go.

I'm going there to see my father,
I'm going there no more to roam,
I'm only going over Jordan,
I'm only going over home.

I know dark clouds will gather 'round me,
I know my way is rough and steep,
Yet golden fields lie just before me,
Where God's redeemed shall ever sleep.

I'm going there to see my mother,
She said she'd meet me when I come,
I'm only going over Jordan,
I'm only going over home.

I'll soon be free from every trial,
My body sleep in the churchyard,
I'll drop the cross of self-denial,
And enter on my great reward.

title: Wade in the Water
origin: Traditional African American spiritual; lyrics first published 1901 in New Jubilee Songs as Sung by the Fisk Jubilee Singers, compiled by Frederick J. Work and John Wesley Work Jr.
note: Associated with Underground Railroad lore — wading in water to throw pursuing dogs off the scent. First commercially recorded by the Sunset Four Jubilee Singers, 1925 (Paramount). The 1901 publication places the text firmly in the public domain.
verses:
Wade in the water,
Wade in the water, children,
Wade in the water,
God's a-going to trouble the water.

See that host all dressed in white,
God's a-going to trouble the water,
The leader looks like the Israelite,
God's a-going to trouble the water.

See that band all dressed in red,
God's a-going to trouble the water,
Looks like the band that Moses led,
God's a-going to trouble the water.

If you don't believe I've been redeemed,
God's a-going to trouble the water,
Just follow me down to Jordan's stream,
God's a-going to trouble the water.

title: Scarborough Fair
origin: Traditional English ballad descended from "The Elfin Knight" (Child Ballad No. 2), traceable to c. 1670; the familiar "parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme" refrain dates to 19th-century Yorkshire versions, printed by Frank Kidson in Traditional Tunes (1891)
note: A centuries-old riddle ballad in which each lover sets the other a series of impossible tasks. Carried to a global audience by Simon & Garfunkel's 1966 arrangement, but the traditional song itself is firmly public domain.
verses:
Are you going to Scarborough Fair?
Parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme;
Remember me to one who lives there,
For once she was a true love of mine.

Tell her to make me a cambric shirt,
Parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme;
Without any seam nor needlework,
Then she shall be a true love of mine.

Tell her to find me an acre of land,
Parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme;
Between the salt water and the sea strand,
Then she shall be a true love of mine.

Tell her to reap it with a sickle of leather,
Parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme;
And gather it all in a bunch of heather,
Then she shall be a true love of mine.

title: Greensleeves
origin: Traditional English song; a broadside ballad "A New Northern Dittye of the Lady Greene Sleeves" was registered at the London Stationers' Company in September 1580
note: One of the oldest continuously sung tunes in the English language, popularly (though without evidence) attributed to Henry VIII. The melody later carried the Christmas carol "What Child Is This?" (1865).
verses:
Alas, my love, you do me wrong,
To cast me off discourteously;
And I have loved you so long,
Delighting in your company.

Greensleeves was all my joy,
Greensleeves was my delight,
Greensleeves was my heart of gold,
And who but my lady Greensleeves.

I have been ready at your hand,
To grant whatever you would crave;
I have both wagered life and land,
Your love and good will for to have.

Greensleeves, now farewell, adieu,
God I pray to prosper thee;
For I am still thy lover true,
Come once again and love me.

title: The Water Is Wide
origin: Traditional Scottish/English folk song assembled from floating verses of the "O Waly, Waly" family (17th century); the modern composite text was shaped by Cecil Sharp's collections in the early 1900s
note: Sharp published a version as "The Water Is Wide" in Folk Songs from Somerset (1906). Built from centuries-old wandering verses, it is a traditional public-domain song, though individual modern arrangements can carry their own copyright.
verses:
The water is wide, I cannot cross o'er,
And neither have I wings to fly.
Give me a boat that can carry two,
And both shall row, my love and I.

Oh, down in the meadows the other day,
A-gathering flowers both fine and gay,
A-gathering flowers both red and blue,
I little thought what love can do.

I leaned my back up against some oak,
Thinking that he was a trusty tree;
But first he bended and then he broke,
And so did my false love to me.

There is a ship and she sails the sea,
She's loaded deep as deep can be,
But not so deep as the love I'm in,
I know not if I sink or swim.

title: Home on the Range
origin: Words from Dr. Brewster M. Higley's poem "My Western Home," published in the Smith County Pioneer (Kansas), 1873; melody by Daniel E. Kelley; words and music first printed together in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads (1910)
note: The official state song of Kansas. Neither Higley nor Kelley registered a timely copyright, and it spread as a folk song with countless variations; it is firmly in the public domain.
verses:
Oh, give me a home where the buffalo roam,
Where the deer and the antelope play,
Where seldom is heard a discouraging word,
And the skies are not cloudy all day.

Home, home on the range,
Where the deer and the antelope play;
Where seldom is heard a discouraging word,
And the skies are not cloudy all day.

Where the air is so pure, the zephyrs so free,
The breezes so balmy and light,
That I would not exchange my home on the range
For all of the cities so bright.

How often at night when the heavens are bright
With the light from the glittering stars,
Have I stood there amazed and asked as I gazed
If their glory exceeds that of ours.

title: Oh My Darling, Clementine
origin: Attributed to Percy Montrose, first published 1884; the melody derives from Henry S. Thompson's 1863 song "Down by the River Liv'd a Maiden"
note: A Western ballad — half lament, half comic — about a miner's daughter lost to the foaming brine. Published in 1884, it has long been in the public domain.
verses:
In a cavern, in a canyon,
Excavating for a mine,
Dwelt a miner, forty-niner,
And his daughter Clementine.

Oh my darling, oh my darling,
Oh my darling Clementine,
You are lost and gone forever,
Dreadful sorry, Clementine.

Light she was and like a fairy,
And her shoes were number nine,
Herring boxes without topses,
Sandals were for Clementine.

Drove she ducklings to the water
Every morning just at nine,
Hit her foot against a splinter,
Fell into the foaming brine.

Ruby lips above the water,
Blowing bubbles soft and fine,
But alas, I was no swimmer,
So I lost my Clementine.

title: Go Down, Moses
origin: Traditional African American spiritual dating from before the Civil War; first published 1872 in the Fisk Jubilee Singers' songbook
note: Its "Let my people go" refrain, drawn from the Book of Exodus, made it a coded anthem of resistance and freedom. Famously recorded by Paul Robeson and Louis Armstrong; anonymous and public domain.
verses:
When Israel was in Egypt's land,
Let my people go!
Oppressed so hard they could not stand,
Let my people go!

Go down, Moses,
Way down in Egypt's land;
Tell old Pharaoh
To let my people go!

Thus saith the Lord, bold Moses said,
Let my people go!
If not, I'll smite your firstborn dead,
Let my people go!

No more shall they in bondage toil,
Let my people go!
Let them come out with Egypt's spoil,
Let my people go!

title: Michael, Row the Boat Ashore
origin: Traditional African American spiritual first noted among freedmen on St. Helena Island, South Carolina, during the Civil War; first published 1867 in Slave Songs of the United States
note: Originated as a rowing song among the Gullah communities of the South Carolina Sea Islands. Revived as a 1961 folk hit by the Highwaymen; the traditional song is public domain.
verses:
Michael, row the boat ashore, hallelujah!
Michael, row the boat ashore, hallelujah!

Sister, help to trim the sail, hallelujah!
Sister, help to trim the sail, hallelujah!

The River Jordan is chilly and cold, hallelujah!
Chills the body but not the soul, hallelujah!

The river is deep and the river is wide, hallelujah!
Milk and honey on the other side, hallelujah!

title: Deep River
origin: Anonymous African American spiritual; first appeared in print in 1876 in J. B. T. Marsh's The Story of the Jubilee Singers: With Their Songs
note: Often called the best-loved of all the spirituals. Harry T. Burleigh's influential 1916–17 concert arrangements brought it to the recital stage; the underlying spiritual is anonymous and public domain.
verses:
Deep river,
My home is over Jordan.
Deep river, Lord,
I want to cross over into campground.

Oh, don't you want to go
To that gospel feast,
That promised land
Where all is peace?

Deep river, Lord,
I want to cross over into campground.
I want to cross over into campground.

title: Aura Lea
origin: Words by W. W. Fosdick, music by George R. Poulton; published 1861
note: A sentimental parlor ballad sung by soldiers on both sides of the Civil War. Its melody was borrowed in 1956 for Elvis Presley's "Love Me Tender" — but the 1861 original is long in the public domain.
verses:
As the blackbird in the spring,
'Neath the willow tree,
Sat and piped, I heard him sing,
Singing Aura Lea.

Aura Lea, Aura Lea,
Maid of golden hair;
Sunshine came along with thee,
And swallows in the air.

In thy blush the rose was born,
Music, when you spake,
Through thine azure eye the morn,
Sparkling seemed to break.

Aura Lea, the bird may flee,
The willow's golden hair
Swing through winter fitfully,
On the stormy air.

title: Wildwood Flower
origin: Descended from "I'll Twine 'Mid the Ringlets," music by Joseph P. Webster with lyrics credited to Maud Irving, published 1860; the text below follows that original
note: Entered the folk repertoire through oral tradition, its lyrics famously garbled over decades, before the Carter Family's landmark 1928 recording. The 1860 source composition is public domain.
verses:
I'll twine 'mid the ringlets of my raven black hair,
The lilies so pale and the roses so fair,
The myrtle so bright with an emerald hue,
And the pale aronatus with eyes of bright blue.

I'll sing and I'll dance, my laugh shall be gay,
I'll cease this wild weeping, drive sorrow away,
Though my heart is now breaking, he never shall know
That his name made me tremble and my pale cheeks to glow.

He told me he loved me, and promised to love,
Through ill and misfortune, all others above,
Another has won him; ah, misery to tell;
He left me in silence, no word of farewell!

He taught me to love him, he called me his flower
That blossomed for him all the brighter each hour;
But I woke from my dreaming, my idol was clay,
My visions of love have all faded away.

title: Blow the Man Down
origin: Traditional halyard shanty of the Black Ball Line packet ships, documented by the 1860s (quoted in the Syracuse Daily Courier, 1867); full text published 1879 and in Laura A. Smith's The Music of the Waters, 1888
note: Sung hauling halyards on the Western Ocean packets running New York to Liverpool. 'Blow' here means to knock a man down — the Black Ball Line was notorious for hard-driving mates. No author was ever identified.
verses:
Come all ye young fellows that follow the sea,
To me way, hay, blow the man down,
And pray pay attention and listen to me,
Give me some time to blow the man down!

I'm a deep water sailor just in from Hong Kong,
To me way, hay, blow the man down,
If you'll give me some grog, I'll sing you a song,
Give me some time to blow the man down!

As I was a-walking down Paradise Street,
To me way, hay, blow the man down,
A pretty young damsel I chanced for to meet,
Give me some time to blow the man down!

So I tailed her my flipper and took her in tow,
To me way, hay, blow the man down,
And yardarm to yardarm away we did go,
Give me some time to blow the man down!

Aboard the Black Baller I first served my time,
To me way, hay, blow the man down,
And on the Black Baller I wasted my prime,
Give me some time to blow the man down!

title: Low Bridge, Everybody Down (Erie Canal)
origin: Thomas S. Allen of Natick, Massachusetts; copyrighted November 1912, published in sheet music by F.B. Haviland Publishing Co., January 1913
note: Also known as "Fifteen Miles on the Erie Canal." Written as the engine-powered Barge Canal was replacing the old mule-towed Erie Canal — a nostalgia piece even when new. Its 1913 publication places it in the public domain.
verses:
I've got a mule, her name is Sal,
Fifteen miles on the Erie Canal,
She's a good old worker and a good old pal,
Fifteen miles on the Erie Canal.

We've hauled some barges in our day,
Filled with lumber, coal, and hay,
And we know every inch of the way
From Albany to Buffalo.

Low bridge, everybody down,
Low bridge, for we're coming to a town,
And you'll always know your neighbor,
You'll always know your pal,
If you've ever navigated on the Erie Canal.

We'd better get along on our way, old gal,
Fifteen miles on the Erie Canal,
'Cause you bet your life I'd never part with Sal,
Fifteen miles on the Erie Canal.

Get up there, mule, here comes a lock,
We'll make Rome 'bout six o'clock,
One more trip and back we'll go
Right back home to Buffalo.

title: Sometimes I Feel Like a Motherless Child
origin: Traditional African American spiritual from the era of slavery; performed by the Fisk Jubilee Singers in the 1870s, earliest known publication 1899 in William E. Barton's Old Plantation Hymns
note: A sorrow song born of families separated by sale under slavery. Carried into the concert repertoire by Paul Robeson and Marian Anderson, and recorded by Odetta, Richie Havens (his Woodstock 'Freedom' improvisation), and Van Morrison.
verses:
Sometimes I feel like a motherless child,
Sometimes I feel like a motherless child,
Sometimes I feel like a motherless child,
A long way from home,
A long way from home.

Sometimes I feel like I'm almost gone,
Sometimes I feel like I'm almost gone,
Sometimes I feel like I'm almost gone,
A long way from home,
A long way from home.

Sometimes I feel like a feather in the air,
Sometimes I feel like a feather in the air,
Sometimes I feel like a feather in the air,
A long way from home,
A long way from home.

True believer,
True believer,
A long way from home,
A long way from home.

title: Wabash Cannonball
origin: Rewritten by William Kindt, 1904, from J. A. Roff's 1882 song "The Great Rock Island Route"
note: A fictional express train celebrated in one of the most-recorded songs in American folk and country music. The Carter Family cut an early version in 1929 and Roy Acuff made it a country standard in 1936; the underlying song was already public domain by then.
verses:
From the great Atlantic Ocean to the wide Pacific shore,
From the queen of flowing mountains to the south belt by the shore,
She's mighty tall and handsome, and known quite well by all,
She's the regular combination called the Wabash Cannonball.

Listen to the jingle, the rumble and the roar,
As she glides along the woodland, o'er the hills and by the shore,
Hear the mighty rush of the engine, hear that lonesome hobo's call,
While traveling through the jungle on the Wabash Cannonball.

She came down from Birmingham one cold December day,
As she rolled into the station, you could hear all the people say:
There's a girl from Tennessee, she is long and she is tall,
She came down from Birmingham on the Wabash Cannonball.

Now the eastern states are dandy, so the western people say,
From New York to St. Louis and Chicago by the way,
From the hills of Minnesota where the rippling waters fall,
No changes can be taken on the Wabash Cannonball.

Here's to Daddy Claxton, may his name forever stand,
And always be remembered in the courts throughout the land,
His earthly race is over and the curtains round him fall,
We'll carry him home to victory on the Wabash Cannonball.

title: Bury Me Not on the Lone Prairie
origin: Adapted from Edwin H. Chapin's 1839 poem "The Ocean Burial" (set to music by George N. Allen); the cowboy version first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: Also known as "The Cowboy's Lament" (a title shared with "Streets of Laredo," though this is a separate song). Reworked from a sailor's dirge — "O bury me not in the deep, deep sea" became the prairie lament. First recorded by Carl T. Sprague in 1926; the song is traditional and public domain.
verses:
"Oh, bury me not on the lone prairie,"
These words came low and mournfully
From the pallid lips of a youth who lay
On his dying bed at the close of day.

"Oh, bury me not on the lone prairie,
Where the wild coyotes will howl o'er me,
Where the buffalo roams the prairie sea,
Oh, bury me not on the lone prairie."

"It matters not, I've oft been told,
Where the body lies when the heart grows cold,
Yet grant, oh grant this wish to me,
Oh, bury me not on the lone prairie."

"I've always wished to be laid when I died
In the little churchyard on the green hillside,
By my father's grave, there let me be,
Oh, bury me not on the lone prairie."

"Oh, bury me not," and his voice failed there,
But we took no heed of his dying prayer,
In a narrow grave just six by three
We buried him there on the lone prairie.

Yes, we buried him there on the lone prairie,
Where the owl all night hoots mournfully,
And the blizzard beats and the wind blows free
O'er his lowly grave on the lone prairie.

title: Down by the Riverside
origin: Traditional African American spiritual with roots before the Civil War; first published 1918 in Plantation Melodies (Rodeheaver Company, Chicago)
note: Also known as "Ain't Gonna Study War No More" — its refrain echoes Isaiah 2:4. It became an anthem of the pacifist and civil rights movements in the 20th century. The 1918 publication and pre-war origins place it firmly in the public domain.
verses:
Gonna lay down my sword and shield,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna lay down my sword and shield,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna study war no more.

I ain't gonna study war no more,
I ain't gonna study war no more,
I ain't gonna study war no more,
I ain't gonna study war no more,
I ain't gonna study war no more,
I ain't gonna study war no more.

Gonna lay down my heavy load,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna lay down my heavy load,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna study war no more.

Gonna put on my long white robe,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna put on my long white robe,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna study war no more.

Gonna meet my dear old mother,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna meet my dear old mother,
Down by the riverside,
Gonna study war no more.

title: Sweet Betsy from Pike
origin: John A. Stone ("Old Put"), published 1858 in Put's Golden Songster, San Francisco; melody borrowed from the English comic song "Villikins and His Dinah"
note: A comic overland-trail ballad of a Pike County couple crossing the plains to California. Written by a Gold Rush entertainer and published in 1858, it has long been in the public domain.
verses:
Oh, don't you remember sweet Betsy from Pike,
Who crossed the wide prairies with her lover Ike,
With two yoke of cattle, a large yellow dog,
A tall Shanghai rooster, and one spotted hog.

Singing too-ra-li, oo-ra-li, oo-ra-li-ay,
Singing too-ra-li, oo-ra-li, oo-ra-li-ay.

One evening quite early they camped on the Platte,
'Twas near by the road on a green shady flat,
Where Betsy, sore-footed, lay down to repose,
With wonder Ike gazed on that Pike County rose.

The Shanghai ran off, and their cattle all died,
That morning the last piece of bacon was fried,
Poor Ike got discouraged, and Betsy got mad,
The dog drooped his tail and looked wondrously sad.

They soon reached the desert, where Betsy gave out,
And down in the sand she lay rolling about,
While Ike in great tears looked on in surprise,
Saying, "Betsy, get up, you'll get sand in your eyes."

They swam the wide rivers and crossed the tall peaks,
And camped on the prairie for weeks upon weeks,
Starvation and cholera, hard work and slaughter,
They reached California in spite of hell and high water.

title: Down in the Valley
origin: Traditional Appalachian/Ozark folk song; collected 1909 by H. M. Belden, first published in the Journal of American Folk-Lore, 1917
note: Also known as "Birmingham Jail." A lilting waltz-time love lament sung from a jail cell. First commercially recorded by Darby and Tarlton in 1927; documented in print a decade earlier and firmly in the public domain.
verses:
Down in the valley, valley so low,
Hang your head over, hear the wind blow,
Hear the wind blow, dear, hear the wind blow,
Hang your head over, hear the wind blow.

Roses love sunshine, violets love dew,
Angels in heaven know I love you,
Know I love you, dear, know I love you,
Angels in heaven know I love you.

If you don't love me, love whom you please,
Throw your arms round me, give my heart ease,
Give my heart ease, dear, give my heart ease,
Throw your arms round me, give my heart ease.

Write me a letter, send it by mail,
Send it in care of the Birmingham jail,
Birmingham jail, dear, Birmingham jail,
Send it in care of the Birmingham jail.

Build me a castle forty feet high,
So I can see her as she rides by,
As she rides by, dear, as she rides by,
So I can see her as she rides by.

title: Camptown Races
origin: Stephen Foster, published February 1850 by F. D. Benteen (as "Gwine to Run All Night, or De Camptown Races")
note: One of Foster's most enduring songs, popularized by Christy's Minstrels. First published in 1850 and long in the public domain in the United States.
verses:
The Camptown ladies sing this song, doo-dah! doo-dah!
The Camptown racetrack five miles long, oh, doo-dah day!
I come down there with my hat caved in, doo-dah! doo-dah!
I go back home with a pocket full of tin, oh, doo-dah day!

Gwine to run all night! Gwine to run all day!
I'll bet my money on the bob-tail nag,
Somebody bet on the bay.

The long-tail filly and the big black horse, doo-dah! doo-dah!
They fly the track and they both cut across, oh, doo-dah day!
The blind horse stickin' in a big mud hole, doo-dah! doo-dah!
Can't touch bottom with a ten-foot pole, oh, doo-dah day!

Old muley cow come onto the track, doo-dah! doo-dah!
The bob-tail fling her over his back, oh, doo-dah day!
Then fly along like a railroad car, doo-dah! doo-dah!
Runnin' a race with a shootin' star, oh, doo-dah day!

See them flyin' on a ten-mile heat, doo-dah! doo-dah!
Round the racetrack, then repeat, oh, doo-dah day!
I win my money on the bob-tail nag, doo-dah! doo-dah!
I keep my money in an old tow-bag, oh, doo-dah day!

title: Battle Hymn of the Republic
origin: Words by Julia Ward Howe, first published in The Atlantic Monthly, February 1862; set to the tune of "John Brown's Body"
note: Howe wrote the verses in November 1861 after visiting a Union Army camp, adapting the marching tune of "John Brown's Body." Published in 1862 and firmly in the public domain.
verses:
Glory, glory, hallelujah!
Glory, glory, hallelujah!
Glory, glory, hallelujah!
His truth is marching on.

In the beauty of the lilies Christ was born across the sea,
With a glory in His bosom that transfigures you and me;
As He died to make men holy, let us die to make men free,
While God is marching on.

title: Tom Dooley
origin: Traditional North Carolina murder ballad based on the 1866 killing of Laura Foster by Tom Dula; an early version was written by local poet Thomas C. Land soon after Dula's 1868 hanging
note: Rooted in a real Wilkes County tragedy. The traditional ballad is public domain; the 1958 Kingston Trio hit was a copyrighted arrangement of this older song, which was preserved in the singing of Frank Proffitt.
verses:
Hang down your head, Tom Dooley,
Hang down your head and cry;
Hang down your head, Tom Dooley,
Poor boy, you're bound to die.

I met her on the mountain,
There I took her life;
Met her on the mountain,
Stabbed her with my knife.

This time tomorrow,
Reckon where I'll be;
Hadn't-a been for Grayson,
I'd-a been in Tennessee.

This time tomorrow,
Reckon where I'll be;
Down in some lonesome valley,
Hangin' from a white oak tree.

title: Nine Pound Hammer
origin: Traditional African American railroad work song of the post-Civil War South; fragments collected in Texas by Gates Thomas as early as 1891 and printed under this title in Dorothy Scarborough's On the Trail of Negro Folk-Songs (1925)
note: A hammering work song kin to the John Henry ballads, its rhythm set the pace of steel-driving crews. Documented in print by 1925 and in the public domain; later bluegrass versions are separate arrangements.
verses:
This nine-pound hammer
Is a little too heavy
For my size, buddy, for my size.

Roll on, buddy,
Don't you roll so slow;
How can I roll
When the wheels won't go?

There ain't one hammer
Down in this tunnel
That can ring like mine, buddy, that can ring like mine.

I'm goin' on the mountain
Just to see my baby,
But I ain't comin' back, Lord, I ain't comin' back.

Take this hammer,
Carry it to the captain,
Tell him I'm gone, buddy, tell him I'm gone.

title: Molly Malone
origin: Traditional Irish; attributed to James Yorkston of Edinburgh, first published in the US (Boston) 1876 and in 'Students' Songs' 1884, and by Francis Brothers and Day, London, 1884
note: Dublin's unofficial anthem, also known as 'Cockles and Mussels.' Though printed with Yorkston's name in the 1880s, it long ago passed into the traditional repertoire and is in the public domain; there is no evidence Molly was a real person.
verses:
In Dublin's fair city,
Where the girls are so pretty,
I first set my eyes on sweet Molly Malone,
As she wheeled her wheelbarrow
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"

Alive, alive, oh!
Alive, alive, oh!
Crying "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"

She was a fishmonger,
But sure 'twas no wonder,
For so were her father and mother before,
And they each wheeled their barrow
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"

She died of a fever,
And no one could save her,
And that was the end of sweet Molly Malone,
But her ghost wheels her barrow
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"

title: The Bonnie Banks o' Loch Lomond
origin: Traditional Scottish; earliest known publication in 'Vocal Melodies of Scotland,' 1841; original author unknown
note: Often read as the lament of a captured Jacobite soldier after Culloden: the 'low road' is the path of the dead, by which his spirit will reach Scotland before his living companion on the 'high road.'
verses:
By yon bonnie banks and by yon bonnie braes,
Where the sun shines bright on Loch Lomond,
Where me and my true love were ever wont to gae,
On the bonnie, bonnie banks o' Loch Lomond.

O ye'll tak' the high road, and I'll tak' the low road,
And I'll be in Scotland afore ye,
But me and my true love will never meet again,
On the bonnie, bonnie banks o' Loch Lomond.

'Twas there that we parted in yon shady glen,
On the steep, steep side o' Ben Lomond,
Where in purple hue the Hieland hills we view,
And the moon comin' out in the gloamin'.

The wee birdies sing and the wildflowers spring,
And in sunshine the waters are sleepin',
But the broken heart it kens nae second spring again,
Though the waefu' may cease frae their greetin'.

title: Steal Away to Jesus
origin: African American spiritual attributed to Wallace and Minerva Willis, Choctaw Territory, before 1862; first published in 'Jubilee Songs' (Fisk University), 1872
note: Widely understood to have carried a hidden message to those planning escape from slavery. Transcribed from Willis's singing and popularized nationally by the Fisk Jubilee Singers.
verses:
Steal away, steal away,
Steal away to Jesus!
Steal away, steal away home,
I ain't got long to stay here.

My Lord, He calls me,
He calls me by the thunder,
The trumpet sounds within-a my soul,
I ain't got long to stay here.

Green trees are bending,
Poor sinner stands a-trembling,
The trumpet sounds within-a my soul,
I ain't got long to stay here.

My Lord, He calls me,
He calls me by the lightning,
The trumpet sounds within-a my soul,
I ain't got long to stay here.

title: The Old Chisholm Trail
origin: Traditional American cowboy song of the 1870s; first published in John A. Lomax, 'Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads,' 1910
note: One of the most-sung trail songs of the open-range era, with a famous 'coma ti yi youpy' refrain and, by cowboy report, dozens of improvised verses. Named for the cattle trail of drover Jesse Chisholm.
verses:
Come along, boys, and listen to my tale,
I'll tell you of my troubles on the old Chisholm trail.
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya, youpy ya,
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya.

I started up the trail October twenty-third,
I started up the trail with the 2-U herd.
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya, youpy ya,
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya.

I'm up in the mornin' before daylight,
And before I sleep the moon shines bright.
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya, youpy ya,
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya.

It's cloudy in the west, a-lookin' like rain,
And my darned old slicker's in the wagon again.
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya, youpy ya,
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya.

Goin' to the boss to draw my roll,
Goin' back South to the girl I love.
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya, youpy ya,
Coma ti yi youpy, youpy ya.

title: Auld Lang Syne
origin: Words by Robert Burns, 1788, adapted from an older Scots folk song; first published in James Johnson's 'Scots Musical Museum,' vol. 5, 1796
note: Sung the world over at the stroke of the New Year. Burns said he took the words 'from an old man's singing'; the now-standard pentatonic tune was paired with them in 1799.
verses:
Should auld acquaintance be forgot,
And never brought to mind?
Should auld acquaintance be forgot,
And auld lang syne!

For auld lang syne, my dear,
For auld lang syne,
We'll tak' a cup o' kindness yet,
For auld lang syne.

We twa hae run about the braes,
And pou'd the gowans fine;
But we've wander'd mony a weary foot,
Sin' auld lang syne.

We twa hae paidl'd in the burn,
Frae mornin' sun till dine;
But seas between us braid hae roar'd
Sin' auld lang syne.

And there's a hand, my trusty fiere,
And gie's a hand o' thine,
And we'll tak' a right guid-willie waught,
For auld lang syne.

title: Simple Gifts
origin: Elder Joseph Brackett Jr., 1848, Shaker community at Alfred, Maine
note: A Shaker dancing song written in 1848. Little known outside Shaker communities until Aaron Copland wove its melody into his ballet score Appalachian Spring (1944); the original song is public domain.
verses:
'Tis the gift to be simple, 'tis the gift to be free,
'Tis the gift to come down where we ought to be,
And when we find ourselves in the place just right,
'Twill be in the valley of love and delight.

When true simplicity is gained,
To bow and to bend we shan't be ashamed,
To turn, turn will be our delight,
Till by turning, turning we come round right.

title: Whiskey in the Jar
origin: Traditional Irish highwayman ballad, thought to date to the 17th century (Roud 533); earliest broadside printings mid-19th century
note: Folklorist Alan Lomax linked the song to the ballad-singing that inspired John Gay's The Beggar's Opera (1728). Popularized worldwide by Thin Lizzy (1972) and Metallica (1998), but the traditional song itself is public domain.
verses:
As I was going over the far-famed Kerry mountains,
I met with Captain Farrell and his money he was counting.
I first produced my pistol and then produced my rapier,
Saying, "Stand and deliver, for you are a bold deceiver."

Musha ring dumma do damma da,
Whack for the daddy-o, whack for the daddy-o,
There's whiskey in the jar.

I counted out his money and it made a pretty penny,
I put it in my pocket and I took it home to Jenny.
She sighed and she swore that she never would deceive me,
But the devil take the women, for they never can be easy.

I went into my chamber all for to take a slumber,
I dreamt of gold and jewels and for sure it was no wonder,
But Jenny drew my charges and she filled them up with water,
Then sent for Captain Farrell to be ready for the slaughter.

'Twas early in the morning before I rose to travel,
Up came a band of footmen and likewise Captain Farrell.
I first produced my pistol, for she stole away my rapier,
But I couldn't shoot the water, so a prisoner I was taken.

title: Casey Jones
origin: Words by T. Lawrence Seibert, music by Eddie Newton; copyrighted 1909 (Southern California Music Co.), based on an earlier ballad by engine-wiper Wallace Saunders
note: Casey Jones died in the 1900 wreck of the Cannonball Express on the Illinois Central; his friend Wallace Saunders made up the first verses. The 1909 published version is public domain in the US.
verses:
Come all you rounders if you want to hear
The story of a brave engineer,
Casey Jones was the rounder's name,
On a six-eight wheeler, boys, he won his fame.

The caller called Casey at half past four,
He kissed his wife at the station door,
Mounted to the cabin with his orders in his hand,
And he took his farewell trip to the promised land.

Casey Jones, mounted to his cabin,
Casey Jones, with his orders in his hand,
Casey Jones, mounted to his cabin,
And he took his farewell trip to the promised land.

"Put in your water and shovel in your coal,
Put your head out the window, watch the drivers roll,
I'll run her till she leaves the rail,
'Cause we're eight hours late with the western mail."

He looked at his watch and his watch was slow,
He looked at the water and the water was low,
He turned to his fireman and this is what he said,
"Boy, we're going to reach 'Frisco, but we'll all be dead."

Casey pulled up that Reno hill,
He tooted for the crossing with an awful shrill,
The switchman knew by the engine's moans,
That the man at the throttle was Casey Jones.

title: Spanish Ladies
origin: Traditional British naval song (Roud 687); earliest record in the 1796 logbook of HMS Nellie, printed in full by Captain Frederick Marryat in his novel Poor Jack (1840)
note: A homeward-bound naval song predating the merchant-sailor shanty era; later borrowed into the shanty repertoire. Famously recited by Quint in Jaws (1975) and used in Master and Commander.
verses:
Farewell and adieu to you, Spanish ladies,
Farewell and adieu to you, ladies of Spain,
For we've received orders for to sail for old England,
But we hope in a short time to see you again.

We'll rant and we'll roar like true British sailors,
We'll rant and we'll roar all on the salt seas,
Until we strike soundings in the channel of old England,
From Ushant to Scilly is thirty-five leagues.

We hove our ship to with the wind from sou'west, boys,
We hove our ship to, deep soundings to take,
'Twas forty-five fathoms with a white sandy bottom,
So we squared our main yard and up channel did make.

The first land we sighted was called the Dodman,
Next Rame Head off Plymouth, off Portsmouth the Wight,
We sailed by Beachy, by Fairlight and Dover,
And then we bore up for the South Foreland light.

Then the signal was made for the grand fleet to anchor,
All in the Downs that night for to lie,
Let go your shank painter, let go your cat stopper,
Haul up your clewgarnets, let tacks and sheets fly.

title: Drill, Ye Tarriers, Drill
origin: Words by Thomas Casey, music by Charles Connolly; first published 1888
note: A comic labor song about the Irish 'tarriers' who drilled and blasted rock for the American railroads. A number-one hit in 1891; published in 1888, it is in the public domain.
verses:
Every morning at seven o'clock
There were twenty tarriers a-working at the rock,
And the boss comes along and he says, "Keep still,
And come down heavy on the cast-iron drill."

And drill, ye tarriers, drill,
Drill, ye tarriers, drill,
Oh, it's work all day for the sugar in your tay,
Down behind the railway,
And drill, ye tarriers, drill, and blast, and fire.

The new foreman was Jean McCann,
By God, he was a blame mean man,
Last week a premature blast went off,
And a mile in the air went big Jim Goff.

When next payday came around,
Jim Goff a dollar short was found,
When he asked what for, came this reply,
"You were docked for the time you were up in the sky."

The boss was a fine man down to the ground,
And he married a lady six feet round,
She baked good bread and she baked it well,
But she baked it hard as the hobs of hell.

title: Yankee Doodle
origin: Traditional; melody older than the words, first printed in the comic opera The Disappointment (New York/Philadelphia, 1767); lyrics assumed their familiar form c. 1775, printed in Glasgow 1782
note: Originally sung by British soldiers to mock colonial 'Yankees,' then embraced by Americans during the Revolution. 'Macaroni' referred to an over-fashionable dandy. Firmly public domain.
verses:
Yankee Doodle went to town,
A-riding on a pony,
Stuck a feather in his cap
And called it macaroni.

Yankee Doodle, keep it up,
Yankee Doodle dandy,
Mind the music and the step,
And with the girls be handy.

Father and I went down to camp,
Along with Captain Gooding,
And there we saw the men and boys
As thick as hasty pudding.

And there was Captain Washington
Upon a slapping stallion,
A-giving orders to his men,
I guess there was a million.

And then the feathers on his hat,
They looked so very fine, ah,
I wanted peskily to get
To give to my Jemima.

title: The Ash Grove
origin: Traditional Welsh air (Llwyn Onn); tune first printed 1802 in Edward Jones's The Bardic Museum; best-known English lyrics by Thomas Oliphant, published 1862
note: One of the most beloved Welsh melodies; a related air appears in John Gay's The Beggar's Opera (1728). Both the 1802 tune and the 1862 English words are public domain.
verses:
The ash grove, how graceful, how plainly 'tis speaking,
The harp through it playing has language for me.
Whenever the light through its branches is breaking,
A host of kind faces is gazing on me.

The friends of my childhood again are before me,
Each step wakes a memory as freely I roam.
With soft whispers laden its leaves rustle o'er me,
The ash grove, the ash grove alone is my home.

Down yonder green valley where streamlets meander,
When twilight is fading, I pensively rove,
Or at the bright noontide in solitude wander
Amid the dark shades of the lonely ash grove.

'Twas there while the blackbird was cheerfully singing
I first met my dear one, the joy of my heart.
Around us for gladness the bluebells were ringing,
Ah, then little thought I how soon we should part.

title: Git Along, Little Dogies
origin: Traditional American cowboy song from the post-Civil War cattle drives; first printed in John A. Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads (1910)
note: Also known as 'Whoopee Ti Yi Yo.' A night-herding song sung to keep cattle calm on the trail; 'dogies' are motherless calves. Public domain as a collected traditional song.
verses:
As I was a-walking one morning for pleasure,
I spied a cowpuncher a-riding along;
His hat was throwed back and his spurs were a-jingling,
And as he approached he was singing this song:

Whoopee ti yi yo, git along, little dogies,
It's your misfortune and none of my own,
Whoopee ti yi yo, git along, little dogies,
For you know Wyoming will be your new home.

It's early in spring that we round up the dogies,
We mark them and brand them and bob off their tails;
We round up our horses, load up the chuck wagon,
Then throw the little dogies out on the long trail.

Your mother she was raised way down in Texas,
Where the jimson weed and the sandburs grow;
Now we'll fill you up on prickly pear and cholla
Till you're ready for the trail to Idaho.

Some boys go up the trail just for pleasure,
But that's where they get it most awfully wrong;
For you haven't any idea the trouble they give us
As we go driving them all along.

title: Shall We Gather at the River
origin: Words and music by Robert Lowry, 1864; first published 1865 as 'Beautiful River' in Happy Voices
note: Written by Baptist minister Robert Lowry in Brooklyn during a summer epidemic. A staple of American revivals and funerals, later used in John Ford Westerns. Long in the public domain.
verses:
Shall we gather at the river,
Where bright angel feet have trod,
With its crystal tide forever
Flowing by the throne of God?

Yes, we'll gather at the river,
The beautiful, the beautiful river;
Gather with the saints at the river
That flows by the throne of God.

On the margin of the river,
Washing up its silver spray,
We will walk and worship ever,
All the happy golden day.

Ere we reach the shining river,
Lay we every burden down;
Grace our spirits will deliver,
And provide a robe and crown.

Soon we'll reach the shining river,
Soon our pilgrimage will cease;
Soon our happy hearts will quiver
With the melody of peace.

title: Little Brown Jug
origin: Written by Joseph Eastburn Winner (as 'Eastburn'), published in Philadelphia, 1869
note: A comic drinking song that endured as a folk standard and gained fresh popularity during Prohibition. Glenn Miller turned it into a 1939 swing hit; the 1869 original is public domain.
verses:
My wife and I lived all alone,
In a little log hut we called our own;
She loved gin and I loved rum,
I tell you what, we'd lots of fun.

Ha, ha, ha, you and me,
Little brown jug, don't I love thee;
Ha, ha, ha, you and me,
Little brown jug, don't I love thee.

'Tis you who makes my friends my foes,
'Tis you who makes me wear old clothes;
Here you are so near my nose,
So tip her up and down she goes.

When I go toiling to my farm,
I take little brown jug under my arm;
I place it under a shady tree,
Little brown jug, 'tis you and me.

If all the folks in Adam's race
Were gathered together in one place,
Then I'd prepare to shed a tear
Before I'd part from you, my dear.

title: The Skye Boat Song
origin: Traditional Gaelic rowing air collected in the 1870s by Anne Campbell MacLeod; lyrics by Sir Harold Boulton, first published 1884 in Songs of the North
note: Recounts Bonnie Prince Charlie's 1746 escape 'over the sea to Skye' with Flora MacDonald after Culloden. The traditional tune and the 1884 (pre-1928) lyric are both public domain in the US.
verses:
Speed, bonnie boat, like a bird on the wing,
Onward, the sailors cry;
Carry the lad that's born to be king
Over the sea to Skye.

Loud the winds howl, loud the waves roar,
Thunderclaps rend the air;
Baffled our foes stand by the shore,
Follow they will not dare.

Though the waves leap, soft shall ye sleep,
Ocean's a royal bed;
Rocked in the deep, Flora will keep
Watch by your weary head.

Many's the lad fought on that day,
Well the claymore could wield,
When the night came, silently lay
Dead on Culloden's field.

Burned are their homes, exile and death
Scatter the loyal men;
Yet, ere the sword cool in the sheath,
Charlie will come again.

title: Down by the Salley Gardens
origin: Poem by W. B. Yeats, published 1889 in The Wanderings of Oisin and Other Poems; set to the traditional air 'The Maids of Mourne Shore' by Herbert Hughes, 1909
note: Yeats built the poem from three half-remembered lines sung by an old woman in County Sligo. Both the 1889 poem and Hughes's 1909 setting are public domain.
verses:
In a field by the river my love and I did stand,
And on my leaning shoulder she laid her snow-white hand.
She bid me take life easy, as the grass grows on the weirs;
But I was young and foolish, and now am full of tears.

title: Make Me a Pallet on the Floor
origin: Traditional; circulating in New Orleans by the turn of the 20th century, first documented in print c. 1908-1911 by folklorist Howard W. Odum
note: Often mistakenly credited to W. C. Handy, who reworked it as 'Atlanta Blues' (1923) — only that arrangement is copyrighted; the traditional song itself was treated as public domain even on its 1925 first recording.
verses:
Make me a pallet on your floor,
Make me a pallet on your floor,
Make it soft, make it low,
So my good gal will never know,
Oh, make me a pallet on your floor.

When I was sick and lying down,
I did not have no friends in town,
For this one reason, if no more,
Just make me a pallet on your floor.

Oh, don't turn a good man from your door,
Oh, don't turn a good man from your door,
He may be poor but he needn't be low,
So make him a pallet on your floor.

The reason I love my pallet on the floor,
The reason I love my pallet on the floor,
Made so easy, way down low,
Where the wind don't blow through the cracks in the floor.

title: Delia
origin: Traditional African American murder ballad based on the Christmas Eve 1900 killing of 14-year-old Delia Green in Savannah, Georgia; collected by Howard Odum 1906-1908 and printed in the Journal of American Folklore, 1911
note: Also known as 'Delia's Gone.' A widely traveled ballad recorded by Blind Willie McTell, Blind Blake, and later Johnny Cash and Bob Dylan. Rooted in a documented real case; the traditional song is public domain.
verses:
Delia, oh Delia, Delia all my life,
If I hadn't have shot poor Delia,
I'd have had her for my wife,
Delia's gone, one more round, Delia's gone.

Delia, oh Delia, how can it be?
You loved all those rounders,
But you never did love me,
Delia's gone, one more round, Delia's gone.

Cooney and his sweetheart sat down to talk,
Cooney drew his pistol,
Began to shoot and balk,
Delia's gone, one more round, Delia's gone.

Cooney went to Atlanta, dressed all in black,
He took the train going down there,
But he never did come back,
Delia's gone, one more round, Delia's gone.

Jailer, oh jailer, jailer, I can't sleep,
For all around my bedside
Poor Delia's ghost does creep,
Delia's gone, one more round, Delia's gone.

title: Leave Her, Johnny
origin: Traditional pump/capstan shanty derived from the c. 1850 song 'Across the Western Ocean'; documented in W. B. Whall's memoir School and Sea Days (1901), recalling the 1860s
note: Reserved for the very end of a voyage, once the ship was moored — the one time a sailor could freely air his complaints about the ship and its officers. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, the times were hard and the wages low,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her,
I'll pack my bag and go below,
And it's time for us to leave her.

Oh, the wind was foul and the sea ran high,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her,
She shipped it green and none went by,
And it's time for us to leave her.

I thought I heard the old man say,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her,
Tomorrow you will get your pay,
And it's time for us to leave her.

The work was hard, the voyage was long,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her,
The seas were high, the gales were strong,
And it's time for us to leave her.

Oh, leave her, Johnny, leave her with a grin,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her,
There's many a worse ship than the one we're in,
And it's time for us to leave her.

title: Row, Row, Row Your Boat
origin: Traditional American round; earliest confirmed publication 1852, credited to Eliphalet Oram Lyte in later 19th-century songbooks
note: One of the most widely sung rounds in the English language, taught in American schools since the 19th century. Long in the public domain.
verses:
Row, row, row your boat,
Gently down the stream,
Merrily, merrily, merrily, merrily,
Life is but a dream.

title: Pop Goes the Weasel
origin: Traditional English dance tune, referenced in print by the 1850s; American verses ("Monkey" verse) added by the 1900s
