Jim Murphy was arrested and then released on bail,
He jumped his bond at Tyler and then went straight to jail,
He sent for Major Jones and had a private talk,
And told how he'd betray poor Sam if he was turned out to walk.

title: Poor Ellen Smith
origin: Traditional North Carolina murder ballad based on the 1892 killing of Ellen Smith near Winston, North Carolina; first commercially recorded 1927 by the Carolina Buddies
note: Rooted in a documented 1892 case in which Peter DeGraff was convicted and hanged for the crime. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Poor Ellen Smith, how she was found,
Shot through the heart, lyin' cold on the ground,
Her clothes were all scattered and thrown on the ground,
And blood marks the spot where poor Ellen was found.

I've been sentenced to die, boys, on the gallows so high,
For killing poor Ellen and now I must die,
I told 'em I loved her, I told 'em to tell,
That I never would harm her, I loved her so well.

They picked up poor Ellen and carried her home,
Beneath the tall pine trees they buried her low,
The birds still are singing, her memory to keep,
While poor Ellen Smith in her cold grave doth sleep.

title: Haul on the Bowline
origin: Traditional short-drag shanty, possibly dating as far back as the era of Sir Francis Drake in the 16th century, though firmly documented aboard sailing ships by the 19th century
note: Possibly the oldest shanty still commonly sung, with some scholars tracing references to the Tudor navy. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Haul on the bowline, the fore and maintop bowline,
Haul on the bowline, the bowline haul!

Haul on the bowline, Kitty is my darling,
Haul on the bowline, the bowline haul!

Haul on the bowline, so early in the morning,
Haul on the bowline, the bowline haul!

title: Whup Jamboree
origin: Traditional homeward-bound capstan shanty, documented aboard sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: A lively homeward-bound shanty, sung with anticipation as a ship neared port at the end of a long voyage. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Now we are in the harbor of Havre de Grace,
Whup jamboree, whup jamboree,
And every man showed a joyful face,
Whup jim bo, whup jim bo.

So we'll pay Paddy Doyle for his boots,
Whup jamboree, whup jamboree,
And we'll all drink brandy and gin,
Whup jim bo, whup jim bo.

Then it's home, dearie, home,
Whup jamboree, whup jamboree,
Oh, don't you hear the old man say,
Whup jim bo, whup jim bo.

title: Long Time Ago
origin: Traditional halyard shanty, documented aboard sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: A gentle, slow-paced halyard shanty with a wandering, easily improvised set of verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
A Yankee ship came down the river,
Blow, boys, blow,
Her masts and yards they shone like silver,
Long time ago.

Who do you think was the captain of her?
Blow, boys, blow,
Old Bully Waterman was the captain of her,
Long time ago.

She's a fast clipper ship and a bully good crew,
Blow, boys, blow,
A down-east Yankee for her captain too,
Long time ago.

title: Listen to the Mockingbird
origin: Words and music by Septimus Winner (under the pen name Alice Hawthorne), published 1855
note: One of the best-selling songs of the 19th century, later used as incidental music in countless silent films and cartoons. Public domain since 1855.
verses:
I'm dreaming now of Hallie, sweet Hallie, sweet Hallie,
I'm dreaming now of Hallie, for the thought of her is one that never dies,
She's sleeping in the valley, the valley, the valley,
She's sleeping in the valley, and the mockingbird is singing where she lies.

Listen to the mockingbird, listen to the mockingbird,
The mockingbird still singing o'er her grave,
Listen to the mockingbird, listen to the mockingbird,
Still singing where the weeping willows wave.

title: Silver Threads Among the Gold
origin: Words by Eben E. Rexford, music by Hart Pease Danks, published 1873
note: A sentimental ballad of enduring love in old age, one of the most popular American songs of the late 19th century. Public domain since 1873.
verses:
Darling, I am growing old,
Silver threads among the gold,
Shine upon my brow today,
Life is fading fast away.

But, my darling, you will be, will be,
Always young and fair to me,
Yes, my darling, you will be,
Always young and fair to me.

When your hair is silver white,
And your cheeks no longer bright,
With the roses of the May,
I will kiss your lips and say,
Oh, my darling, mine alone, alone,
You have never older grown.

title: Free at Last
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A spiritual of hope and deliverance, later echoed in Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1963 "I Have a Dream" speech, which quoted the spiritual's refrain. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Free at last, free at last,
Thank God almighty, I'm free at last,
Free at last, free at last,
Thank God almighty, I'm free at last.

Way down yonder in the graveyard walk,
I thank God I'm free at last,
Me and my Jesus going to meet and talk,
I thank God I'm free at last.

On my knees when the light passed by,
I thank God I'm free at last,
Thought my soul would rise and fly,
I thank God I'm free at last.

title: Great Day
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: An energetic spiritual anticipating the day of judgment with joy rather than fear. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Great day, great day, the righteous marching,
Great day, God's going to build up Zion's walls.

This is the day of jubilee,
God's going to build up Zion's walls,
The Lord has set His people free,
God's going to build up Zion's walls.

Chariot rode on the mountain top,
God's going to build up Zion's walls,
My God spoke and the chariot stopped,
God's going to build up Zion's walls.

title: I'm Gonna Sing When the Spirit Says Sing
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: A joyful spiritual of spontaneous, spirit-led worship, sung with the same call-and-response structure across many verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I'm gonna sing when the spirit says sing,
I'm gonna sing when the spirit says sing,
And obey the spirit of the Lord.

I'm gonna shout when the spirit says shout,
I'm gonna shout when the spirit says shout,
And obey the spirit of the Lord.

I'm gonna pray when the spirit says pray,
I'm gonna pray when the spirit says pray,
And obey the spirit of the Lord.

title: The Trooper and the Maid
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, documented in broadside form from the 18th century, closely related to Child Ballad No. 299
note: A ballad of a soldier's brief romance with a maid before he rides off to war, widely collected across Scotland and Ireland. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
A trooper lad cam' here last night,
Wi' his blankets, boots, and a',
He's laid his pistols at my bed head,
And he's turned his face to the wa'.

'Oh, ye'll gang oot and haud my horse,
And hold him weel, I say,
And dinna let him touch the corn,
For I maun march at break o' day.'

'Oh, come again, my trooper lad,
When a' your wars are done,
And if ye stay till I grow auld,
I'll wait till ye come hame.'

title: Shortnin' Bread
origin: Traditional African American folk song, documented in print by the 1900s; became a national hit after James Whitcomb Riley published a poem drawing on it in 1900
note: A widely sung children's clapping and dance song. Presented here with the standard verses in common circulation today, without heavier period dialect found in some early printings.
verses:
Mama's little baby loves shortnin', shortnin',
Mama's little baby loves shortnin' bread,
Mama's little baby loves shortnin', shortnin',
Mama's little baby loves shortnin' bread.

Put on the skillet, put on the lid,
Mama's gonna make a little shortnin' bread,
That ain't all she's gonna do,
Mama's gonna make a little coffee too.

Three little children lyin' in bed,
Two were sick and the other 'most dead,
Sent for the doctor and the doctor said,
'Give those children some shortnin' bread.'

title: Weevily Wheat
origin: Traditional Appalachian play-party song, likely originating in 18th-century Scotland; documented in American folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A play-party dance song with roots possibly connected to Jacobite-era Scottish tunes, brought to Appalachia by Scots-Irish settlers. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, I won't have none of your weevily wheat,
I won't have none of your barley,
I'll take some flour in half an hour,
To bake a cake for Charley.

Charley he's a nice young man,
Charley he's a dandy,
Charley he's a nice young man,
He buys the girls some candy.

The higher up the cherry tree,
The riper grows the cherries,
The more you hug and kiss the girls,
The sooner they will marry.

title: Fly Around My Pretty Little Miss
origin: Traditional Appalachian play-party and square-dance song, documented in southern mountain collections by the early 20th century
note: A lively square-dance play-party song, sung a cappella for dances in communities without instrumental music. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Fly around, my pretty little miss,
Fly around, my daisy,
Fly around, my pretty little miss,
You almost drive me crazy.

I don't care what the old folks say,
Home ain't home no longer,
I don't care what the old folks say,
Home ain't home no longer.

Little bit of meat and a whole lot of bone,
What's the matter with this old gal?
Made my living with a hoein' corn,
What's the matter with this old gal?

title: Bought Me a Cat
origin: Traditional American cumulative folk song, documented in Appalachian and Ozark folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A cumulative farmyard song in which the singer acquires a new animal each verse, layering all the previous animal sounds together. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I bought me a cat, my cat pleased me,
I fed my cat under yonder tree,
My cat says fiddle-i-fee.

I bought me a hen, my hen pleased me,
I fed my hen under yonder tree,
My hen says chimmy-chuck, chimmy-chuck,
My cat says fiddle-i-fee.

I bought me a duck, my duck pleased me,
I fed my duck under yonder tree,
My duck says quack, quack,
My hen says chimmy-chuck, chimmy-chuck,
My cat says fiddle-i-fee.

title: The Old Rugged Cross
origin: Words and music by George Bennard, completed and copyrighted 1913
note: Written by a Methodist evangelist over several months of meditation in Michigan and Wisconsin. One of the most beloved American gospel hymns. Public domain since 1913.
verses:
On a hill far away stood an old rugged cross,
The emblem of suffering and shame,
And I love that old cross where the dearest and best,
For a world of lost sinners was slain.

So I'll cherish the old rugged cross,
Till my trophies at last I lay down,
I will cling to the old rugged cross,
And exchange it some day for a crown.

In that old rugged cross, stained with blood so divine,
A wondrous beauty I see,
For 'twas on that old cross Jesus suffered and died,
To pardon and sanctify me.

title: Sweet Hour of Prayer
origin: Words attributed to William Walford, published 1845; music by William Bradbury, 1859
note: Walford, a blind English lay preacher, is said to have composed the words in his head and dictated them to a visiting American minister. Public domain since 1845.
verses:
Sweet hour of prayer, sweet hour of prayer,
That calls me from a world of care,
And bids me at my Father's throne,
Make all my wants and wishes known.

In seasons of distress and grief,
My soul has often found relief,
And oft escaped the tempter's snare,
By thy return, sweet hour of prayer.

Sweet hour of prayer, sweet hour of prayer,
Thy wings shall my petition bear,
To Him whose truth and faithfulness
Engage the waiting soul to bless.

title: Just As I Am
origin: Words by Charlotte Elliott, published 1835; most common tune by William Bradbury, 1849
note: Written by an English invalid poet, this became one of the most-used hymns for evangelical altar calls, famously associated with Billy Graham's crusades in the 20th century. Public domain since 1835.
verses:
Just as I am, without one plea,
But that Thy blood was shed for me,
And that Thou bidst me come to Thee,
O Lamb of God, I come, I come.

Just as I am, and waiting not
To rid my soul of one dark blot,
To Thee whose blood can cleanse each spot,
O Lamb of God, I come, I come.

Just as I am, though tossed about
With many a conflict, many a doubt,
Fightings and fears within, without,
O Lamb of God, I come, I come.

title: Softly and Tenderly
origin: Words and music by Will L. Thompson, published 1880
note: A gentle invitational hymn, reportedly a favorite of evangelist Dwight L. Moody, who is said to have called Thompson a greater hymn-writer than himself. Public domain since 1880.
verses:
Softly and tenderly Jesus is calling,
Calling for you and for me,
See, on the portals He's waiting and watching,
Watching for you and for me.

Come home, come home,
Ye who are weary, come home,
Earnestly, tenderly, Jesus is calling,
Calling, O sinner, come home.

Why should we tarry when Jesus is pleading,
Pleading for you and for me,
Why should we linger and heed not His mercies,
Mercies for you and for me?

title: Thomas the Rhymer
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 37, connected to the legend of 13th-century poet Thomas of Erceldoune; earliest printed text appears in Robert Jamieson's Popular Ballads, 1806
note: A ballad in which a mortal poet is taken by the Queen of Elfland and granted the gift (and curse) of a tongue that cannot lie. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
True Thomas lay on Huntlie bank,
A ferlie he spied wi' his e'e,
And there he saw a lady bright,
Come riding down by the Eildon tree.

Her skirt was o' the grass-green silk,
Her mantle o' the velvet fyne,
At ilka tett of her horse's mane,
Hung fifty siller bells and nine.

'Harp and carp, Thomas,' she said,
'Harp and carp along wi' me,
And if ye dare to kiss my lips,
Sure of your body I will be.'

title: Hind Horn
origin: Traditional Scottish/English ballad, Child Ballad No. 17, drawing on the medieval romance of King Horn, dated to the 13th century
note: A ballad in which a returning lover uses a magic ring to test his sweetheart's faithfulness before revealing himself. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
In Scotland there was a babe born,
With a hey lillelu and a how lo lan,
And his name it was called young Hind Horn,
And the birk and the broom blooms bonnie.

Seven long years he served the king,
And it's a' for the sake of his daughter Jean.

'Here is a ring to thee I'll give,
And as lang as this ring keeps new,
You may know that I am a lover true.'

title: The Broomfield Hill
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 43, documented from at least the 17th century
note: A ballad in which a woman outwits a knight's plan to seduce her while she sleeps, using magic herbs to escape unharmed. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
There was a knight and a lady bright,
Had a true tryst at the broom,
The one to come at twelve o'clock,
The other to be there at noon.

'I'll gar my true-love eat a leaf,
That springs on yonder tree,
And it shall gar him sleep sound,
Until I come and gae.'

And when she came to broomfield hill,
She found her love asleep,
With a silver belt about his head,
And a broom-cow at his feet.

title: I Want Jesus to Walk with Me
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A quiet, personal spiritual asking for divine companionship through life's trials. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I want Jesus to walk with me,
I want Jesus to walk with me,
All along my pilgrim journey,
I want Jesus to walk with me.

In my trials, Lord, walk with me,
In my trials, Lord, walk with me,
When my heart is almost breaking,
I want Jesus to walk with me.

When I'm troubled, walk with me,
When I'm troubled, walk with me,
When my head is bowed in sorrow,
I want Jesus to walk with me.

title: Certainly Lord
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: A call-and-response spiritual affirming faith with the emphatic reply 'certainly, Lord.' Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Have you got good religion? Certainly, Lord!
Have you got good religion? Certainly, Lord!
Have you got good religion? Certainly, Lord!
Certainly, certainly, certainly, Lord!

Have you been baptized? Certainly, Lord!
Have you been baptized? Certainly, Lord!
Have you been baptized? Certainly, Lord!
Certainly, certainly, certainly, Lord!

Do you love everybody? Certainly, Lord!
Do you love everybody? Certainly, Lord!
Do you love everybody? Certainly, Lord!
Certainly, certainly, certainly, Lord!

title: Oh, Freedom
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, emerging soon after the end of the Civil War in 1865, adapted from the earlier spiritual "Before I'd Be a Slave" (documented 1899)
note: A defiant post-emancipation spiritual, later a central anthem of the 1950s-60s Civil Rights Movement, famously performed by Odetta and sung on the Selma to Montgomery march. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, freedom, oh, freedom,
Oh, freedom over me, over me,
And before I'd be a slave,
I'll be buried in my grave,
And go home to my Lord and be free.

No more moanin', no more moanin',
No more moanin' over me, over me,
And before I'd be a slave,
I'll be buried in my grave,
And go home to my Lord and be free.

No more weepin', no more weepin',
No more weepin' over me, over me,
And before I'd be a slave,
I'll be buried in my grave,
And go home to my Lord and be free.

title: The Coast of High Barbary
origin: Traditional English sea ballad, referencing the Barbary pirates of North Africa; documented in broadside form from the 17th-18th centuries
note: An old sea ballad of a naval encounter with Barbary corsairs, sung aboard both merchant and naval vessels for generations. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Look ahead, look astern, look the weather and the lee,
Blow high, blow low, and so sailed we,
I spy a wreck to windward, and a lofty ship to lee,
A-sailing down along the coast of the High Barbary.

'O are you a pirate or a man-o'-war?' cried we,
Blow high, blow low, and so sailed we,
'O no, I'm not a pirate but a man-o'-war,' cried he,
A-sailing down along the coast of the High Barbary.

'Then back your topsails, man-o'-war,' cried we,
Blow high, blow low, and so sailed we,
But instead he fired a broadside right into our lee,
A-sailing down along the coast of the High Barbary.

title: Heave Away, My Johnny
origin: Traditional capstan shanty, documented aboard emigrant ships crossing the Atlantic in the mid-19th century
note: A capstan shanty associated with the transatlantic emigrant trade of the 1800s, sung while weighing anchor for the long crossing to America. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
As I walked out one morning fair,
Heave away, my Johnny, heave away,
To view the fields and the flowers there,
Away, my Johnny boys, we're all bound to go.

I heard a maiden that did complain,
Heave away, my Johnny, heave away,
That she'd never see her true love again,
Away, my Johnny boys, we're all bound to go.

We're bound away to New York town,
Heave away, my Johnny, heave away,
To see the girls all dance around,
Away, my Johnny boys, we're all bound to go.

title: The Bucking Bronco
origin: Traditional American cowgirl song, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A wistful ballad, unusually sung from a woman's perspective, warning other girls away from charming cowboys. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
My love is a rider, wild broncos he breaks,
Though he's promised to quit it, just for my sake,
He ropes and he ties, he throws the great steers,
He learned the trade early, he's rode all his years.

Now all you young maidens, where'er you reside,
Beware of the cowboy who swings the rawhide,
He'll court you and pet you and leave you to go,
In the spring up the trail on his bucking bronco.

title: The Band Played On
origin: Words by John F. Palmer, music by Charles B. Ward, published 1895
note: A waltz-time hit satirizing high society, with its famous 'Casey would waltz with the strawberry blonde' refrain. Public domain since 1895.
verses:
Matt Casey formed a social club that beat all creation,
They ordered a room and bought a drum for a large celebration,
To gain admission you had to wear a badge or discard,
And if you got gay, they'd throw you away, quicker than a Bowery guard.

Casey would waltz with the strawberry blonde,
And the band played on,
He'd glide 'cross the floor with the girl he adored,
And the band played on,
His brain was so loaded it nearly exploded,
The poor girl would shake with alarm,
He'd ne'er leave the girl with the strawberry curls,
And the band played on.

title: A Bird in a Gilded Cage
origin: Words by Arthur J. Lamb, music by Harry Von Tilzer, published 1900
note: One of the biggest sheet-music hits of its era, telling of a woman who married for wealth rather than love. Public domain since 1900.
verses:
The ballroom was filled with fashion's throng,
It shone with a thousand lights,
And there was a woman who passed along,
The fairest of all the sights.

She's only a bird in a gilded cage,
A beautiful sight to see,
You may think she's happy and free from care,
She's not, though she seems to be,
'Tis sad when you think of her wasted life,
For youth cannot mate with age,
And her beauty was sold for an old man's gold,
She's a bird in a gilded cage.

title: In the Shade of the Old Apple Tree
origin: Words by Harry H. Williams, music by Egbert Van Alstyne, published 1905
note: A nostalgic waltz recalling a rural courtship, one of the best-selling songs of 1905. Public domain since 1905.
verses:
In the shade of the old apple tree,
Where the love in your eyes I could see,
When the voice that I heard, like the song of a bird,
Seemed to whisper sweet music to me.

I could hear the dull buzz of the bee,
In the blossoms as you said to me,
With a heart that is true, I'll be waiting for you,
In the shade of the old apple tree.

title: The Old Oaken Bucket
origin: Words by Samuel Woodworth, published 1817 (as a poem); set to music by various composers, most commonly George Kiallmark, 1826
note: A sentimental poem recalling the well-bucket of the poet's childhood farm, one of the most widely reprinted American poems of the 19th century before being set to music. Public domain since 1817.
verses:
How dear to this heart are the scenes of my childhood,
When fond recollection presents them to view,
The orchard, the meadow, the deep-tangled wildwood,
And every loved spot which my infancy knew.

The old oaken bucket, the iron-bound bucket,
The moss-covered bucket, which hung in the well.

That moss-covered vessel I hailed as a treasure,
For often at noon, when returned from the field,
I found it the source of an exquisite pleasure,
The purest and sweetest that nature can yield.

title: Long, Long Ago
origin: Words and music by Thomas Haynes Bayly, published 1833
note: A gentle English parlor ballad of remembered love, still occasionally used in the melody for other songs and nursery adaptations. Public domain since 1833.
verses:
Tell me the tales that to me were so dear,
Long, long ago, long, long ago,
Sing me the songs I delighted to hear,
Long, long ago, long ago.

Now you are come, all my grief is removed,
Let me forget that so long you have roved,
Let me believe that you love as you loved,
Long, long ago, long ago.

Do you remember the path where we met?
Long, long ago, long, long ago,
Ah, yes, you told me you ne'er would forget,
Long, long ago, long ago.

title: Sweet Genevieve
origin: Words by George Cooper, music by Henry Tucker, published 1869
note: A sentimental ballad of mourning for a lost love, remaining popular in barbershop quartet repertoire well into the 20th century. Public domain since 1869.
verses:
Sweet Genevieve, sweet Genevieve,
The days may come, the days may go,
But still the hands of memory weave,
The blissful dreams of long ago.

Sweet Genevieve, sweet Genevieve,
My days of youth seem all a dream,
Oh, I loved you then, as I love you still,
Since first you dawned on my sight.

The old church bell had pealed its last,
While happy feet were gathered at the door,
Five winters have come and gone,
Since I stood beside you before.

title: The Preacher and the Bear
origin: Words and music by Joe Arzonia (credited as Arzonia Fields), copyrighted 1904
note: A comic novelty hit about a preacher treed by a bear during an illicit Sunday hunting trip, one of the best-selling records of the early 1900s (notably by Arthur Collins). Public domain since 1904.
verses:
A preacher went out a-huntin', 'twas on one Sunday morn,
With his gun upon his shoulder, he says, 'I'm going for game.'
He spied a great big grizzly bear a-standin' by a tree,
He grabbed his gun and away he run, up a tree he flew like the very devil.

Oh, Lord, you delivered Daniel from the lion's den,
Also delivered Jonah from the belly of the whale, and then,
Three Hebrew children from the fiery furnace,
So the Good Book do declare,
Oh, Lord, if you can't help me,
For goodness sake don't help that bear.

title: Ida, Sweet as Apple Cider
origin: Words and music by Eddie Munson and Eddie Leonard, published 1903
note: A cheerful courting song that remained a standard vaudeville and barbershop number for decades. Public domain since 1903.
verses:
I've got a girl, she's on my mind,
All the time, she's some kind,
She's got a way, believe me, it's a way that's all her own,
When it comes to lovin' I'll bet she's a champion.

Ida, sweet as apple cider,
Sweeter than all I know,
Come out in the moonlight, and see with me,
The silv'ry moon a-glow,
I've got a longin' for a home, sweet home,
With someone just like you,
Ida, sweet as apple cider,
I'm gettin' stuck on you.

title: Sweet William's Ghost
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 77, first printed in 1740 in Allan Ramsay's Tea-Table Miscellany
note: A ghost ballad in which a deceased lover returns to ask his sweetheart to release him from his troth so his spirit may rest. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
There came a ghost to Margaret's door,
With many a grievous groan,
And aye he tirled at the pin,
But answer made she none.

'Is this my father Philip?
Or is't my brother John?
Or is't my true-love Willie,
From Scotland new come home?'

''Tis not thy father Philip,
Nor yet thy brother John,
But 'tis thy true-love Willie,
From Scotland new come home.'

title: King Henry
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 32, with roots possibly connected to medieval romance tales; documented from the 18th century
note: A strange ballad in which a hospitable king is tested by a monstrous, supernatural guest who transforms into a beautiful woman once properly served. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
Let never a man a-wooing wend,
That lacketh things three,
A store of gold, an open heart,
And full of charity.

And this was seen of King Henry,
For he lay burd-alone,
And he's ta'en him to a haunted hunt's hall,
Was seven miles frae a town.

He's chased the deer now him before,
And the roe down by the den,
Till the fattest buck in a' the flock,
King Henry he has slain.

title: Get on Board, Little Children
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, using the Underground Railroad and gospel train imagery; documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th century
note: A gospel train spiritual, its imagery widely understood to carry a coded message of escape during the era of slavery. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Get on board, little children,
Get on board, little children,
Get on board, little children,
There's room for many a more.

The gospel train's a-comin',
I hear it just at hand,
I hear the car wheels rumblin',
And rollin' through the land.

I hear the train a-comin',
She's comin' round the curve,
She's loosened all her steam and brakes,
And strainin' every nerve.

title: Fix Me, Jesus
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: A quiet, contemplative spiritual asking for spiritual readiness before death. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Fix me, Jesus, fix me,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me,
Fix me for my long white robe,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me.

Fix me for my journey home,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me,
Fix me for my starry crown,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me.

Fix me for the judgment day,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me,
Won't you fix me for that day,
Fix me, Jesus, fix me.

title: City Called Heaven
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A spiritual of deep longing and isolation, imagining a heavenly city as refuge from earthly sorrow. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I am a poor pilgrim of sorrow,
I'm left in this wide world alone,
No hope for tomorrow,
I've started to make heaven my home.

Sometimes I am tossed and driven, Lord,
Sometimes I don't know where to roam,
I've heard of a city called heaven,
I've started to make it my home.

My mother has gone to pure glory,
My father's still walkin' in sin,
My sisters and brothers won't own me,
Because I am tryin' to get in.

title: Storm Along (Mister Stormalong)
origin: Traditional halyard and capstan shanty, likely referencing a legendary giant sailor figure of American maritime folklore; documented aboard ships by the mid-19th century
note: Sometimes sung as a funeral or dirge shanty for a fellow sailor, honoring the legendary 'Old Stormalong,' a folkloric giant of the sea. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Old Stormy he is dead and gone,
To my aye, storm along,
Old Stormy he is dead and gone,
Aye, aye, aye, Mister Storm along.

We'll dig his grave with a silver spade,
To my aye, storm along,
His shroud of the finest silk was made,
Aye, aye, aye, Mister Storm along.

We'll lower him down with a golden chain,
To my aye, storm along,
And every day we'll watch his grave,
Aye, aye, aye, Mister Storm along.

title: Sacramento
origin: Traditional capstan shanty from the California Gold Rush era, documented aboard ships sailing 'round the Horn to San Francisco in the 1850s
note: A rousing shanty sung by sailors racing to the California goldfields, referencing the boomtown of Sacramento. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, around Cape Horn we're bound to go,
To my hoodah, to my hoodah,
Around Cape Horn through the sleet and snow,
Hoodah, to my hoodah, day.

We're bound for the town of Sacramento,
To my hoodah, to my hoodah,
With a bran' new suit for the growing season,
Hoodah, to my hoodah, day.

So it's blow, ye winds, heigh-ho,
For Californ-i-o,
There's plenty of gold, so I've been told,
On the banks of the Sacramento.

title: The Alabama (Roll, Alabama, Roll)
origin: Traditional Civil War-era sea ballad commemorating the Confederate raider CSS Alabama, sunk off Cherbourg, France in 1864
note: A shanty recounting the exploits and sinking of the Confederate commerce raider Alabama, later collected as a folk song by sailors of many nations. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
When the Alabama's keel was laid,
Roll, Alabama, roll,
'Twas laid in the yard of Jonathan Laird,
Oh, roll, Alabama, roll.

She was built at Birkenhead, far away,
Roll, Alabama, roll,
She was built at Birkenhead, far away,
Oh, roll, Alabama, roll.

Down south she went a-cruisin' about,
Roll, Alabama, roll,
And many a Yankee ship laid low,
Oh, roll, Alabama, roll.

title: Sioux Indians
origin: Traditional American pioneer ballad, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A pioneer-era travel ballad recounting a wagon train's encounter with Sioux warriors on the westward trail. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
We crossed the broad prairie, our purpose to find,
A country far distant and pleasant in clime,
We were led by a pilot, expecting to gain,
A fertile new region, free from care and pain.

In our little band there were forty and four,
And all of us reached this fair Colorado shore,
Our pilot he tried, our way to lead on,
While the red skins pursued us, our lives for to take.

We heard of Sioux Indians all out on the plain,
A-killing poor drivers and burning their grain,
They killed men and women, and children also,
As we were informed by the man from Ohio.

title: Pick a Bale of Cotton
origin: Traditional African American work song from the 19th-century American South, with earliest documented field recordings made in 1933 by folklorists John and Alan Lomax
note: A rhythmic cotton-picking work song of unknown, anonymous authorship, later given a well-known personal arrangement by Lead Belly. The traditional song predates and is distinct from any single performer's specific version.
verses:
Oh, Lordy, pick a bale of cotton,
Oh, Lordy, pick a bale a day,
Oh, Lordy, pick a bale of cotton,
Oh, Lordy, pick a bale a day.

Me and my partner can pick a bale of cotton,
Me and my partner can pick a bale a day,
Me and my partner can pick a bale of cotton,
Me and my partner can pick a bale a day.

Gonna pick a bale of cotton, gonna pick it by myself,
Gonna pick a bale of cotton, gonna pick it by myself,
Gonna pick a bale of cotton, gonna pick it by myself.

title: Old Blue
origin: Traditional American folk song about a hunting hound, documented in southern folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A well-loved song about a faithful hunting dog, later covered by many folk revival artists. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I had a dog and his name was Blue,
Betcha five dollars he's a good dog too,
Saddle up, boys, and go with me,
We're gonna chase this possum up a hollow tree.

Old Blue treed and I went to see,
There was a possum up that 'simmon tree,
Old Blue barked and I went to look,
There was old Blue treed on a possum's track.

Old Blue died and he died so hard,
He shook the ground in my back yard,
Dug his grave with a silver spade,
Lowered him down with a golden chain.

title: My Wild Irish Rose
origin: Words and music by Chauncey Olcott, published 1899
note: Written by an Irish-American vaudeville and Broadway star, this became one of the most enduring Irish-American parlor ballads. Public domain since 1899.
verses:
If you listen, I'll sing you a sweet little song,
Of a flow'r that's now dead, yet dearer to me,
Yes, dearer to me than all of its mates,
Though each holds a beauty that no other shares.

My wild Irish Rose,
The sweetest flow'r that grows,
You may search ev'rywhere,
But none can compare,
With my wild Irish Rose.

My wild Irish Rose,
The dearest flow'r that grows,
And some day for my sake,
She may let me take,
The bloom from my wild Irish Rose.

title: When Irish Eyes Are Smiling
origin: Words by Chauncey Olcott and George Graff Jr., music by Ernest Ball, published 1912
note: One of the most enduring Irish-American songs, written for the Broadway show The Isle o' Dreams. Public domain since 1912.
verses:
There's a tear in your eye,
And I'm wondering why,
For it never should be there at all,
With such power in your smile,
Sure a stone you'd beguile,
So there's never a teardrop should fall.

When Irish eyes are smiling,
Sure, 'tis like a morn in spring,
In the lilt of Irish laughter,
You can hear the angels sing,
When Irish hearts are happy,
All the world seems bright and gay,
And when Irish eyes are smiling,
Sure, they steal your heart away.

title: Meet Me Tonight in Dreamland
origin: Words by Beth Slater Whitson, music by Leo Friedman, published 1909
note: A gentle romantic ballad by the same songwriting team behind 'Let Me Call You Sweetheart.' Public domain since 1909.
verses:
Softly the leaves of the roses,
Falling upon the ground,
Sweetly the wind of the summer,
In the treetops sound.

Meet me tonight in dreamland,
Under the silv'ry moon,
Meet me tonight in dreamland,
Where love's sweet roses bloom,
Come with the love-light gleaming,
In your dear eyes of blue,
Meet me in dreamland,
Sweet dreamy dreamland,
There let my dreams come true.

title: Lonesome Road Blues (Going Down the Road Feeling Bad)
origin: Traditional American folk-blues song, documented in Appalachian and southern collections by the early 20th century, first commercially recorded 1923 by Henry Whitter
note: A widely traveled lament song, part of a large family of related 'going down the road' traditional songs. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I'm going down this road feeling bad, Lord, Lord,
I'm going down this road feeling bad,
I'm going down this road feeling bad, bad, bad,
And I ain't gonna be treated this way.

I'm going where the water tastes like wine, Lord, Lord,
I'm going where the water tastes like wine,
I'm going where the water tastes like wine,
And I ain't gonna be treated this way.

Two dollar shoes hurt my feet, Lord, Lord,
Two dollar shoes hurt my feet,
Two dollar shoes hurt my feet, feet, feet,
And I ain't gonna be treated this way.

title: The Little Log Cabin in the Lane
origin: Words and music by Will S. Hays, published 1871
note: A sentimental ballad recalling a humble childhood home, one of the most popular songs of its era and a favorite of early country and bluegrass musicians. Public domain since 1871.
verses:
Oh, the old log cabin, tumblin' down, tumblin' down,
Oh, the old log cabin, tumblin' down,
The rats and the mice, they are playin' cards,
In the little log cabin in the lane.

The chimney's fell down, and it's stopped up the door,
With the coon dog barkin' 'neath the sycamore,
The hinges are rusted and the roof has caved in,
In the little log cabin in the lane.

But it's dearer to me than the mansions so fine,
For it's there that my heart, it will always incline,
And I'll never forget those old days of mine,
In the little log cabin in the lane.

title: Poor Boy
origin: Traditional African American folk-blues song, documented in Southern folk collections of the early 20th century
note: A wandering-verse blues song shared widely among early 20th-century folk and blues singers, its lyrics floating freely between related songs. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Poor boy, long ways from home,
Poor boy, long ways from home,
Poor boy, long ways from home,
A long ways from home.

Don't the clouds look lonesome, cross the deep blue sea,
Don't the clouds look lonesome, cross the deep blue sea,
Don't my baby look the sweetest, in the world to me.

Sun's gonna shine in my back door someday,
Sun's gonna shine in my back door someday,
The wind's gonna change and blow my blues away.

title: Get Up and Bar the Door
origin: Traditional Scottish comic ballad, Child Ballad No. 275, documented from the 18th century
note: A comic ballad in which a stubborn husband and wife's silent standoff over who should shut the door lets thieves walk right in. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
It fell about the Martinmas time,
And a gay time it was then,
When our goodwife got puddings to make,
And she's boiled them in the pan.

The wind sae cauld blew south and north,
And blew into the floor,
Quoth our goodman to our goodwife,
'Get up and bar the door.'

'My hand is in my hussyfskep,
Goodman, as ye may see,
And it should not be barred this hundred year,
It's no be barred for me.'

title: The Three Ravens
origin: Traditional English ballad, first printed in Thomas Ravenscroft's Melismata, 1611
note: A gentle, chivalric ballad in which a slain knight is faithfully guarded by his hawks, hounds, and a grieving doe, denying the ravens their expected feast. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
There were three ravens sat on a tree,
Down a down, hay down, hay down,
There were three ravens sat on a tree,
With a down,
There were three ravens sat on a tree,
They were as black as they might be,
With a down derry, derry, derry, down, down.

The one of them said to his mate,
'Where shall we our breakfast take?'
'Down in yonder green field,
There lies a knight slain under his shield.'

'His hounds they lie down at his feet,
So well they can their master keep,
His hawks they fly so eagerly,
There's no fowl dare him come nigh.'

title: My Lord, What a Morning
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th century
note: A solemn spiritual anticipating the day of final judgment, later given a well-known concert arrangement by Harry T. Burleigh. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
My Lord, what a morning,
My Lord, what a morning,
Oh, my Lord, what a morning,
When the stars begin to fall.

You'll hear the trumpet sound,
To wake the nations underground,
Looking to my God's right hand,
When the stars begin to fall.

You'll hear the sinner cry,
To wake the nations underground,
Looking to my God's right hand,
When the stars begin to fall.

title: Sit Down, Servant
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A spiritual imagining the exhausted laborer finally granted rest and honor in heaven. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Sit down, servant, sit down,
Sit down, servant, sit down,
Sit down, servant, sit down,
I can't sit down.

My soul's so happy that I can't sit down,
Can't sit down, can't sit down,
My soul's so happy that I can't sit down,
I can't sit down.

Talkin' about a time in heaven,
Can't sit down, can't sit down,
Going to sit right down and rest a little while,
I can't sit down.

title: Done Made My Vow
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: A determined spiritual affirming a pledge to remain faithful through to the very end of life's journey. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Done made my vow to the Lord,
And I never will turn back,
I will go, I shall go,
To see what the end will be.

I told my Jesus it would be all right,
If He changed my name,
I told my Jesus it would be all right,
If He changed my name.

There is something within me,
That holdeth the reins,
I will go, I shall go,
To see what the end will be.

title: The Dreadnought
origin: Traditional forecastle ballad celebrating the record-breaking packet ship Dreadnought, which sailed the Liverpool-New York run in the 1850s-60s
note: A boastful sailors' ballad celebrating a genuinely famous fast packet ship, nicknamed the 'Wild Boat of the Atlantic.' Traditional and public domain.
verses:
There's a saucy wild packet, a packet of fame,
She belongs to New York and the Dreadnought's her name,
She's bound to the westward where the strong winds do blow,
Bound away in the Dreadnought, to the westward we'll go.

Now the time's fast approaching we sail o'er the sea,
All ye friends and relations, pray keep up for me,
But a health to the Dreadnought and all her brave crew,
To bold Captain Samuels and his officers too.

So it's now we are sailing on the ocean so wide,
Where the deep and blue waters roll fast by her side,
So it's now we are sailing for old England's shore,
And through the Dreadnought's power, boys, our tricks will be o'er.

title: Shallow Brown
origin: Traditional halyard shanty, likely with roots in the Caribbean or American maritime trade; documented aboard sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: A haunting, melancholic shanty addressed to a departing sailor, among the more musically distinctive of the traditional shanty repertoire. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, Shallow, Shallow Brown,
You're going to leave me,
Oh, Shallow, Shallow Brown,
You're going away.

Oh, Shallow Brown, don't you hear me calling,
Oh, Shallow, Shallow Brown,
You're going away.

I'm bound away this very day,
Oh, Shallow, Shallow Brown,
I'm bound away to leave you,
Oh, Shallow, Shallow Brown.

title: Paddy Doyle's Boots
origin: Traditional short-drag shanty, sung for a single sharp pull such as hoisting a heavy sail; documented aboard sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: One of the shortest traditional shanties, used for a single powerful heave rather than sustained hauling. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
To me way, hey, ay, we'll all throw dirt,
And we'll all throw dirt at Paddy Doyle's boots,
We'll all throw dirt, and we'll all throw dirt,
And we'll all throw dirt at Paddy Doyle's boots.

title: Ten Thousand Cattle
origin: Traditional American cowboy song, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A cowboy lament about a devastating cattle-drive loss, sung with the weary resignation typical of range ballads. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Ten thousand cattle straying,
All gone astray,
We circled all day,
But we circled in vain,
Not a hoof, not a horn,
Of my darling I could find,
For the Texas fever it left them behind.

Went out on the round-up,
And found nary a steer,
The wolves and the coyotes,
Had left nothing but the tears,
I'm goin' to leave old Texas now,
They've got no use for the longhorn cow.

title: The Trail to Mexico
origin: Traditional American cowboy trail song, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A cowboy ballad of heartbreak on a long cattle drive south of the border, one of many trail songs collected in the early 20th century range-song revival. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I made up my mind to change my way,
And quit my crowd that was so gay,
To leave my native home for a while,
And travel West for many a mile.

'Twas all in the merry month of May,
When I started for Texas far away,
I left my darling girl behind,
She said her heart was only mine.

With a herd of longhorns I did go,
Down to a place called Mexico,
I was in hopes to change my mind,
And leave my darling girl behind.

title: Oh, Dem Golden Slippers
origin: Words and music by James A. Bland, published 1879
note: Written by James Bland, one of the most successful Black songwriters of the 19th century, this became a jubilant standard associated with Philadelphia's Mummers Parade tradition. Public domain since 1879. One verse from the original sheet music, containing offensive racial dialect, is omitted here.
verses:
Oh, my golden slippers am laid away,
'Cause I don't 'spect to wear 'em till my weddin' day,
And my long-tail coat, that I loved so well,
I will wear up in the chariot in the morn'.

Oh, dem golden slippers, oh, dem golden slippers,
Golden slippers I'm goin' to wear, because they look so neat,
Oh, dem golden slippers, oh, dem golden slippers,
Golden slippers I'm goin' to wear to walk the golden street.

title: In the Baggage Coach Ahead
origin: Words and music by Gussie L. Davis, published 1896
note: Written by one of the most successful Black Tin Pan Alley songwriters of the era, this tearjerker about a widower and his motherless baby was one of the biggest hits of the 1890s. Public domain since 1896.
verses:
On a dark stormy night, as the train rattled on,
All the passengers had gone to bed,
Except one young man with a babe in his arms,
Who sat there with a bowed down head.

In the baggage coach ahead,
In the baggage coach ahead,
Nobody knew of the sorrow and woe,
Of that baby's mother, so pale and dead,
As the train rolled swiftly ahead.

title: Break the News to Mother
origin: Words and music by Charles K. Harris, published 1897
note: Written by the composer of 'After the Ball,' this dying-soldier tearjerker became newly popular during the Spanish-American War the following year. Public domain since 1897.
verses:
While the shot and shell were screaming, upon the battlefield,
The boys in blue were fighting, their noble flag to shield,
Came a cry from their brave captain, 'Look, boys, our flag is down,
Who'll volunteer to save it from disgrace?'

'Just break the news to mother, she knows how dear I love her,
And tell her not to wait for me, for I'm not coming home,
Just say there is no other, can take the place of mother,
Then kiss her dear sweet lips for me, and break the news to her.'

title: Charles Guiteau
origin: Traditional American broadside ballad, first appearing shortly after the July 1881 assassination of President James A. Garfield; adapted from the earlier 1858 execution ballad "James A. Rogers" (Laws E11)
note: A confession ballad sung from the assassin's imagined point of view, part of a family of similarly structured 'gallows confession' songs reused for different real crimes. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Come all you tender Christians,
Wherever you may be,
And likewise pay attention,
To these few lines from me.

My name is Charles Guiteau,
My name I'll ne'er deny,
I leave my aged parents,
In sorrow for to die.

Oh, little did I think,
While in my youthful bloom,
I'd be carried to the scaffold,
To meet my fatal doom.

title: The Boston Burglar
origin: Traditional American ballad, likely of Irish or Anglo-American broadside origin; documented in folk collections since the late 19th century
note: A widely traveled ballad of a young burglar sentenced to prison, sung with the same wistful regret found in many transportation and prison ballads. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I was born in Boston city, a place you all know well,
Brought up by honest parents, the truth to you I'll tell,
Brought up by honest parents, and reared most tenderly,
Until I became a roving man, at the age of twenty-three.

My character was taken then, and I was sent to jail,
My friends they tried to clear me, but nothing could avail,
The jury found me guilty, the clerk he wrote it down,
The judge he passed the sentence, I was bound for Charlestown.

So all you jolly fellows, a warning take by me,
And shun all evil company, wherever you may be,
For if you don't you're sure to rue it, until your dying day,
Like me you'll waste your youthful days in Charlestown far away.

title: Rye Whiskey
origin: Traditional Appalachian folk song, likely descended from older English/Scots-Irish drinking song traditions; documented in southern folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A widely varied drinking song with an enormous number of floating, often improvised verses across its many regional versions. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Rye whiskey, rye whiskey, rye whiskey I cry,
If I don't get rye whiskey, I surely will die.

I've been to England, I've been to Rome,
But I'll drink my rye whiskey when I get back home.

If the ocean was whiskey and I was a duck,
I'd swim to the bottom and never come up.

title: Hop High, Ladies
origin: Traditional American play-party and square-dance song, documented in southern folk collections by the early 20th century
note: Also known as 'The Cake Walk into Town.' A lively square-dance play-party song sung a cappella for community dances. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Hop high, ladies, the cake's all dough,
Why don't you make like a-Molly Bawn?
Hop high, ladies, the cake's all dough,
You needn't mind the weather if the wind don't blow.

Some folks say that a preacher won't steal,
I caught two in my cornfield,
One had a bushel, one had a peck,
One had a roastin' ear tied around his neck.

My true love's a blue-eyed daisy,
If I get well, I'll go plumb crazy,
Hop high, ladies, three in a row,
Hop high, ladies, don't you know.

title: Young Beichan (Lord Bateman)
origin: Traditional English/Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 53, possibly connected to medieval legends of Gilbert Becket; documented in broadside form from the 18th century
note: A ballad in which a lord, imprisoned abroad, is freed by a foreign noblewoman who later crosses the sea to find him on his wedding day. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
Lord Bateman was a noble lord,
A noble lord of high degree,
He shipped himself all aboard of a ship,
Some foreign country for to see.

He sailed east, he sailed west,
Until he came to famed Turkey,
Where he was taken and put in prison,
Until his life was quite weary.

'Have you got houses, have you got land,
Or does Northumberland belong to thee?
What would you give to the fair young lady,
That out of prison would set you free?'

title: The Twa Corbies
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 26, first printed in 1803 in Sir Walter Scott's Minstrelsy of the Scottish Border
note: A stark, unsentimental Scottish counterpart to the gentler English "Three Ravens," in which two crows discuss feasting on a fallen knight, forgotten already by his hawk, hound, and lady. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
As I was walking all alane,
I heard twa corbies making a mane,
The tane unto the tither say,
'Where sall we gang and dine today?'

'In behint yon auld fail dyke,
I wot there lies a new-slain knight,
And naebody kens that he lies there,
But his hawk, his hound, and lady fair.'

'His hound is to the hunting gane,
His hawk to fetch the wild-fowl hame,
His lady's ta'en another mate,
So we may mak our dinner sweet.'

title: Rise and Shine
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, retelling the story of Noah's ark; documented in spiritual and children's church song collections of the early 20th century
note: A lively spiritual recounting the story of Noah, popular in both Black church tradition and children's Sunday school songbooks. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Rise and shine, and give God the glory, glory,
Rise and shine, and give God the glory, glory,
Rise and shine, and give God the glory, glory,
Children of the Lord.

So Noah built him, he built him an arky, arky,
Noah built him, he built him an arky, arky,
Built it out of gopher barky, barky,
Children of the Lord.

The animals, the animals, they came in by twosies, twosies,
Animals, animals, they came in by twosies, twosies,
Elephants and kangaroosies, roosies,
Children of the Lord.

title: Dry Bones (Dem Bones)
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, drawing on Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones; documented in spiritual collections, popularized in James Weldon Johnson's 1928 arrangement
note: A rhythmic spiritual tracing the human skeleton bone by bone, drawing on a vivid image from the Book of Ezekiel. The underlying traditional spiritual predates the 1928 arrangement and is public domain.
verses:
Toe bone connected to the foot bone,
Foot bone connected to the heel bone,
Heel bone connected to the ankle bone,
Now hear the word of the Lord.

Dem bones, dem bones, gonna walk around,
Dem bones, dem bones, gonna walk around,
Dem bones, dem bones, gonna walk around,
Now hear the word of the Lord.

Ezekiel connected dem dry bones,
Ezekiel connected dem dry bones,
Ezekiel connected dem dry bones,
Now hear the word of the Lord.

title: Great Getting-Up Morning
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A dramatic spiritual describing the trumpet call of judgment day, sung with building intensity across its many verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
In that great getting-up morning,
Fare thee well, fare thee well,
In that great getting-up morning,
Fare thee well, fare thee well.

There's a better day a-coming,
Fare thee well, fare thee well,
Gabriel's gonna blow his trumpet,
Fare thee well, fare thee well.

Gonna blow it loud and lonesome,
Fare thee well, fare thee well,
Gonna wake the nations, wake 'em early,
Fare thee well, fare thee well.

title: The Greenland Whale Fisheries
origin: Traditional English/American whaling ballad, documented in broadside form as early as the 1720s, referencing the historic Greenland whale hunting grounds
note: One of the oldest documented whaling ballads in the English language, describing the loss of a whale, boat, and crew in the dangerous Arctic hunting grounds. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
'Twas in eighteen hundred and fifty-three,
And of June the thirteenth day,
That our gallant ship her anchor weighed,
And for Greenland sailed away, brave boys,
And for Greenland sailed away.

The lookout in the crosstrees stood,
With his spyglass in his hand,
'There's a whale, there's a whale, there's a whalefish,' he cried,
'And she blows at every span, brave boys,
And she blows at every span.'

Our boat was struck, and the line paid out,
But she gave a flourish with her tail,
The boat capsized and four men were drowned,
And we never caught that whale, brave boys,
And we never caught that whale.

title: Blow, Ye Winds
origin: Traditional American whaling shanty, documented aboard New England whaling ships by the mid-19th century
note: A whaling recruitment song satirizing the false promises made to entice young men into the brutal whaling trade. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
'Tis advertised in Boston, New York, and Buffalo,
Five hundred brave Americans, a-whaling for to go,
Singing, blow, ye winds in the morning,
And blow, ye winds, high-o!
Clear away your running gear,
And blow, ye winds, high-o!

They send you to New Bedford, that famous whaling port,
And give you to some landsharks, to board and fit you out,
They tell you of the clipper ships a-running in and out,
And say you'll take five hundred sperm before you're six months out.

It's now we're out to sea, my boys, the wind comes on to blow,
One half the watch is sick on deck, the other half below,
But as for me, I'm not sick, but I'm so bold as I can stand,
I'm going up aloft, my boys, to hand, reef, and to steer.

title: Windy Old Weather
origin: Traditional English fishing song, associated with the herring fleets of East Anglia; documented in fishing communities by the 19th century
note: A gentle work song sung by herring fishermen, expressing weary resignation at the hardships of stormy weather and uncertain catches. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, it's windy old weather, boys, it's stormy old weather,
When the wind blows we'll all pull together,
Oh, it's windy old weather, boys, it's stormy old weather,
When the wind blows we'll all pull together.

And the poor fisherman, he goes sailing,
All out on the deep, briny sea,
While his wife and his children are waiting,
For him to come home safely.

So we'll haul in the nets in the morning,
And we'll haul in the nets in the night,
For it's windy old weather, boys, stormy old weather,
When the wind blows we'll all pull together.

title: Diamond Joe
origin: Traditional American cowboy/cattle song, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A cowhand's wry, complaint-filled ballad about working for a demanding ranch boss named Diamond Joe. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I used to work for Diamond Joe,
He fed me on burned beans and dough,
Joe, Joe, come and get my old gray hoss,
Saddle him up, I'm the devil to cross.

Diamond Joe, Diamond Joe,
Come and get your old gray hoss,
Diamond Joe, Diamond Joe,
Come and get your old gray hoss.

He worked me in the sun and rain,
And fed me nothin' but a corn and grain,
Diamond Joe, you'd better come,
Before I take my leave for home.

title: Jawbone
origin: Traditional African American work song, likely from the plantation and post-Civil War South; documented in early folk song collections of the late 19th century
note: A rhythmic call-and-response work and dance song, its odd title possibly referencing a jawbone used as a percussion instrument. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Jawbone eat and jawbone talk,
Jawbone eat with a knife and fork,
Jawbone, jawbone, where you been?
Round the world and back again.

Jawbone walkin', jawbone talkin',
Jawbone eatin' with a knife and fork,
Jawbone, jawbone, don't you cry,
You'll be a mule by and by.

title: Shuck That Corn Before You Eat
origin: Traditional African American work song sung during autumn corn-shucking gatherings; documented in Southern folk collections of the 19th century
note: A rhythmic harvest-time work song sung communally at corn-shucking gatherings, a common seasonal social and labor event across the rural South. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Shuck that corn before you eat,
Shuck that corn before you eat,
Ev'rybody sing till the moon shine bright,
Shuck that corn before you eat.

Pile it high and pile it low,
Shuck that corn before you eat,
Work all night till the roosters crow,
Shuck that corn before you eat.

Turn out here and shuck this corn,
Shuck that corn before you eat,
Biggest crop since I was born,
Shuck that corn before you eat.

title: The Letter Edged in Black
origin: Words and music by Hattie Nevada, published 1897
note: A tearjerker ballad about receiving news of a parent's death by mail, later recorded by the Carter Family and many country artists. Public domain since 1897.
verses:
I was standing at my window yesterday morning,
Without a thought of worry or of care,
When I saw the postman coming up the pathway,
With a swift and steady step I did not know.

I opened it and read it, oh, my God, the sorrow,
It caused the tears from my eyes to overflow,
My father wrote me these few lines and said, 'Be brave, my boy,
Your dear mother's gone, her sorrows we all know.'

There's a whitened saint above who watches o'er us,
There's a golden crown for all our tears and pain,
But it's hard to lose a mother while we're waiting,
For a letter edged in black to come again.

title: Two Little Girls in Blue
origin: Words and music by Charles Graham, published 1893
note: A sentimental ballad of two sisters, once inseparable, whose lives take opposite turns after a broken romance. Public domain since 1893.
verses:
An old man gazed on a photograph,
In a locket he'd worn for years,
His nephew asked him the reason why,
That picture had caused him tears.

'I'll tell you a story,' the old man said,
'Of a love that has been true,
Two little girls in blue, lad,
Two little girls in blue.

'They were sisters, we were brothers,
And learned to love the two,
And one little girl in blue, lad,
Won your heart from you.'

title: Little Annie Rooney
origin: Words and music by Michael Nolan, published 1890
note: A cheerful courting song popular on both sides of the Atlantic, later lending its title to a series of silent film comedies. Public domain since 1890.
verses:
I'm not a girl who cares for gold or silver,
For I've a love, I love him very dear,
He works so hard the whole day long,
To earn enough to make our home more cheer.

She is my sweetheart, I'm her beau,
She's my Annie, I'm her Joe,
Soon we'll marry, never to part,
Little Annie Rooney is my sweetheart.

title: The Foggy, Foggy Dew
origin: Traditional English folk song, catalogued as Roud 558, with roots tracing to at least the 18th century; distinct from the unrelated Irish ballad of similar name about the 1916 Easter Rising
note: A wistful ballad of a weaver's brief romance, told with restraint through euphemism rather than explicit detail. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
When I was a bachelor, I lived all alone,
I worked at the weaver's trade,
And the only, only thing that I ever did wrong,
Was to woo a fair young maid.

I wooed her in the wintertime,
And in the summer, too,
And the only, only thing that I did that was wrong,
Was to keep her from the foggy, foggy dew.

One night she came to my bedside,
When I was fast asleep,
She laid her head upon my bed,
And she began to weep.

title: Devilish Mary
origin: Traditional Appalachian comic ballad, likely of English origin; documented in southern mountain folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A comic complaint song about marrying a hot-tempered woman, in the same vein as "The Wife Wrapt in Wether's Skin." Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I wish I was a young girl again,
But a young girl I'll never be,
Till apples grow on an orange tree,
Devilish Mary won't marry me.

I courted her for seven long years,
At last we come to be married,
I wish't my heart had a-broke before,
Ever I saw devilish Mary.

She'll scratch your eyes and tear your hair,
And throw the fryin' pan at you too,
Devilish Mary was her name,
And I'll never marry again.

title: The Old Gray Mare
origin: Melody adapted from the 1858 minstrel song "Down in Alabam'," with the familiar comic lyrics documented by the early 20th century
note: A widely known comic dance song, its melody borrowed from an older minstrel-era tune, now sung with entirely different, family-friendly lyrics. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, the old gray mare, she ain't what she used to be,
Ain't what she used to be, ain't what she used to be,
The old gray mare, she ain't what she used to be,
Many long years ago.

Many long years ago, many long years ago,
The old gray mare, she ain't what she used to be,
Many long years ago.

She used to jump the fences that she can't jump now,
Can't jump now, can't jump now,
She used to jump the fences that she can't jump now,
Many long years ago.

title: Angelina Baker
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1850 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: A lively banjo tune written for the minstrel stage, later absorbed into the old-time fiddle and banjo repertoire as an instrumental standard. Public domain since 1850.
verses:
Way down on the Mississippi floating,
Everything was there so gay,
I once did love a pretty little girl,
And her name it was Angelina Baker.

Angelina Baker, Angelina Baker,
Oh, I once did love a pretty little girl,
And her name it was Angelina Baker.

Angelina Baker's gone,
She left me here to weep and mourn,
Why she left me I don't know,
I'll try to sing my song.

title: Tam Lin
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 39, documented from at least the late 18th century, with roots in older fairy-lore traditions
note: One of the most celebrated Scottish ballads, in which a young woman braves the Queen of Fairies to win back her mortal lover, transformed into monstrous shapes as she holds fast to him. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
O I forbid you, maidens a',
That wear gowd on your hair,
To come or gae by Carterhaugh,
For young Tam Lin is there.

Janet has kilted her green kirtle,
A little aboon her knee,
And she has broded her yellow hair,
A little aboon her bree,
And she's awa' to Carterhaugh,
As fast as she can hie.

'O tell me, tell me, Tam Lin,' she said,
'For's sake that died on tree,
If e'er ye was in holy chapel,
Or christendom did see?'

'They'll turn me in your arms, lady,
Into an esk and adder,
But hold me fast, and fear me not,
I am your bairn's father.'

title: Lady Isabel and the Elf Knight
origin: Traditional English/Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 4, with related versions found across most of Europe, likely dating to the medieval era
note: A ballad in which a clever young woman outwits a serial-killing suitor and turns his own trap back upon him. One of the most widely distributed ballads in the world, found in dozens of European languages. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
Fair lady Isabel sits in her bower sewing,
Aye as the gowans grow gay,
There she heard an elf-knight blawing his horn,
The first morning in May.

'If I had yon horn that I hear blawing,
And yon elf-knight to sleep in my bosom.'
This maiden had scarcely these words spoken,
Till in at her window the elf-knight has luppen.

'It's a very strange matter, fair maiden,' said he,
'I canna' blaw my horn but ye call on me,
But will ye go to yon greenwood side?
If ye canna' gang, I will cause you to ride.'

title: The Farmer's Curst Wife
origin: Traditional English/Scottish comic ballad, Child Ballad No. 278, documented from the 18th century
note: A comic ballad in which the Devil carries off a farmer's shrewish wife, only to find her too much trouble even for Hell, and returns her. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
There was an old man who lived in a wood,
As you shall plainly see,
He said he could do more work in a day,
Than his wife could do in three.

The Devil he came to the farmer one day,
'One of your family I must have now,
It's not your oldest son that I crave,
But your old scolding wife I now must have.'

When she got there, the little devils they run,
Some with a club and some with a pitchfork,
She up with her foot and kicked fourteen more,
Out through the gates before her.

title: Ain't That Good News
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented by John and Alan Lomax in a 1934 Library of Congress field recording in Huntsville, Texas
note: A joyful spiritual anticipating heavenly reward. Distinct from Sam Cooke's 1964 secular pop song of the same title, which was a later reworking of this older traditional gospel song.
verses:
I've a crown up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I've a crown up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I'm-a gonna lay down this world,
Gonna shoulder up my cross,
Gonna take it home to my Jesus,
Ain't that good news!

I've a harp up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I've a harp up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I'm-a gonna lay down this world,
Gonna shoulder up my cross,
Gonna take it home to my Jesus,
Ain't that good news!

I've a robe up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I've a robe up in the Kingdom,
Ain't that good news,
I'm-a gonna lay down this world,
Gonna shoulder up my cross,
Gonna take it home to my Jesus,
Ain't that good news!

title: Jesus on the Main Line
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century
note: A spiritual using the then-new technology of the telephone as a metaphor for direct communication with God through prayer. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Jesus on the main line, tell Him what you want,
Jesus on the main line, tell Him what you want,
Jesus on the main line, tell Him what you want,
Call Him up and tell Him what you want.

If you need more patience, tell Him what you want,
If you need more patience, tell Him what you want,
If you need more patience, tell Him what you want,
Call Him up and tell Him what you want.

If you're sick and afflicted, tell Him what you want,
If you're sick and afflicted, tell Him what you want,
If you're sick and afflicted, tell Him what you want,
Call Him up and tell Him what you want.

title: Elijah Rock
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the early 20th century, drawing on the story of the prophet Elijah
note: A driving, rhythmic spiritual celebrating the prophet Elijah's ascension to heaven in a fiery chariot. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Elijah rock, shout, shout,
Elijah rock, comin' up, Lord,
Elijah rock, shout, shout,
Elijah rock, comin' up, Lord.

Satan's mad, and I am glad,
He missed that soul he thought he had,
Elijah rock, shout, shout,
Elijah rock, comin' up, Lord.

I never been to heaven but I've been told,
Streets up there are paved with gold,
Elijah rock, shout, shout,
Elijah rock, comin' up, Lord.

title: Hanging Johnny
origin: Traditional halyard shanty, documented aboard sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: A darkly comic halyard shanty in which a sailor nicknamed 'Hanging Johnny' claims to hang everything from ropes to relatives, sung with tongue-in-cheek gallows humor. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, they call me Hanging Johnny,
Away, boys, away,
Oh, they call me Hanging Johnny,
So hang, boys, hang.

They says I hanged my granny,
Away, boys, away,
They says I hanged my granny,
So hang, boys, hang.

First I hanged her cat, boys,
Away, boys, away,
Then I hanged her dog, boys,
So hang, boys, hang.

title: The Flying Cloud
origin: Traditional Irish-American ballad about a fictional pirate slave ship, documented in broadside and folk collections from the mid-19th century
note: A long confession ballad, told from the gallows, of a sailor drawn into piracy and the slave trade aboard the ship Flying Cloud. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
My name is Edward Hollander, as you might understand,
I was born in the county of Waterford, in Erin's happy land,
When I was young and in my prime, and fortune on me smiled,
My parents reared me tenderly, I being their only child.

My father bound me to a trade in Waterford's own town,
He bound me to a cooper there, by the name of William Brown,
I served my master faithfully, for eighteen months or more,
Then I shipped on board the Ocean Queen, bound for Bermuda's shore.

It was after the boarding of the Ocean Queen, we set sail for the Gold Coast shore,
And the fifty-five poor slaves, my boys, from their native land we bore,
We made them walk upon the deck, all in a rank and file,
Better for them if they'd died at home, than to work in Cuba's isle.

title: Little Joe the Wrangler
origin: Words by N. Howard "Jack" Thorp, published 1908 in his collection Songs of the Cowboys, one of the earliest cowboy song anthologies
note: A tribute ballad to a young orphan cowhand who dies in a stampede saving the herd. Public domain since 1908.
verses:
Little Joe the wrangler, he'll wrangle never more,
His days with the remuda they are done,
'Twas a year ago last April he joined our happy band,
A little Texas stray and all alone.

'Twas long late in the evening he rode up to our camp,
And asked if he could get a job,
He said he'd lost his parents, a poor and orphan lad,
And he was driven from his home by a mob.

'Twas a dark and stormy night, we was on a bed ground,
The herd was millin' round and round,
When we heard the cattle rushing, we heard a hellish sound,
And Joe he went to the leaders side.

title: Comin' Thro' the Rye
origin: Words by Robert Burns, published 1782, set to a traditional Scottish tune
note: A cheerful Scottish folk song about chance meetings in a rye field, later immortalized as the title reference in J.D. Salinger's The Catcher in the Rye. Public domain since 1782.
verses:
Gin a body meet a body,
Comin' thro' the rye,
Gin a body kiss a body,
Need a body cry?

Ilka lassie has her laddie,
Nane, they say, hae I,
Yet all the lads they smile on me,
When comin' thro' the rye.

Gin a body meet a body,
Comin' frae the town,
Gin a body kiss a body,
Need a body frown?

title: Sweet Rosie O'Grady
origin: Words and music by Maude Nugent, published 1896
note: Written by one of the few successful women Tin Pan Alley songwriters of her era, this became a beloved Irish-American courting ballad. Public domain since 1896.
verses:
A simple little maiden, from the wild and woolly west,
She thinks she is the queen of all, the queen of all the rest,
Oh, she's not built like a palace, she has manners plain and true,
She wears no jewels, no silks and satins, and yet a bird in curls.

Sweet Rosie O'Grady, my dear little rose,
She's my steady lady, most everyone knows,
And when we are married, how happy we'll be,
I love sweet Rosie O'Grady, and Rosie O'Grady loves me.

title: Ta-ra-ra Boom-de-ay
origin: Words and music by Henry J. Sayers, published 1891
note: One of the biggest transatlantic hits of the early 1890s, its nonsense refrain became so ubiquitous it was parodied and referenced for decades afterward. Public domain since 1891.
verses:
A smart and stylish girl you see,
Belle of good society,
Not too strict, but rather free,
Yet as right as right can be.

Ta-ra-ra boom-de-ay,
Ta-ra-ra boom-de-ay,
Ta-ra-ra boom-de-ay,
Ta-ra-ra boom-de-ay.

Never mind, whate'er they say,
It doesn't matter, anyway,
Whatever people do or say,
I live my life the modern way.

title: Comrades
origin: Words and music by Felix McGlennon, published 1887
note: A sentimental music-hall ballad of lifelong friendship, popular on both sides of the Atlantic. Public domain since 1887.
verses:
Comrades, comrades, ever since we were boys,
Sharing each other's sorrows, sharing each other's joys,
Comrades when manhood was dawning, faithful whate'er may betide,
When danger threatened, my darling old comrade, was there at my side.

Comrades, comrades,
Ever since we were boys,
Sharing each other's sorrows,
Sharing each other's joys.

title: Hammer Ring
origin: Traditional African American railroad and prison work song, documented by folklorists in Southern work camps in the early 20th century
note: A hammering work song from the same family as "John Henry" and "Nine Pound Hammer," sung to coordinate steel-driving crews. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Hammer ring, oh, hammer ring,
Hammer ring, oh, hammer ring,
Can't you hear that hammer ring?
Can't you hear that hammer ring?

This old hammer, killed John Henry,
But it won't kill me, boys, it won't kill me,
This old hammer, killed John Henry,
But it won't kill me.

Take this hammer, throw it in the river,
Gonna throw it in the river, gonna throw it in the river,
Take this hammer, throw it in the river,
It rings like gold, boys, it rings like gold.

title: One Potato, Two Potato
origin: Traditional English/American counting-out rhyme, documented in American children's folklore collections by the early 20th century
note: A widely known counting-out rhyme used by children to select who is 'it' in playground games. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
One potato, two potato,
Three potato, four,
Five potato, six potato,
Seven potato, more.

title: The Rakes of Mallow
origin: Traditional Irish tune, documented in print by the mid-18th century, associated with the fashionable spa town of Mallow, County Cork
note: A lively Irish dance tune celebrating the carefree young gentlemen ('rakes') who once frequented the resort town of Mallow. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Beauing, belling, dancing, drinking,
Breaking windows, damning, sinking,
Ever raking, never thinking,
Live the rakes of Mallow.

Spending faster than it comes,
Beating waiters, bailiffs, duns,
Live the rakes of Mallow.

One time nought but claret drinking,
Then like politicians thinking,
To raise the sinking funds when sinking,
Live the rakes of Mallow.

title: The Croppy Boy
origin: Traditional Irish ballad commemorating the 1798 Irish Rebellion, with the familiar version first printed by William McBurney ("Carroll Malone") in 1845
note: A ballad of a young rebel captured and executed during the 1798 uprising, named for the cropped hairstyle worn by United Irishmen sympathizers. Public domain since its 1845 printing.
verses:
It was early, early in the spring,
The birds did whistle and sweetly sing,
Changing their notes from tree to tree,
And the song they sang was old Ireland free.

It was early, early in the night,
The yeoman cavalry gave me a fright,
The yeoman cavalry was my downfall,
And I was taken by Lord Cornwall.

'Twas in the guard-house where I was laid,
And in a parlor where I was tried,
My sentence passed and my courage low,
When to Dungannon I was forced to go.
